answer:
Yes. There isn’t a single definitive study on *your exact choice* (“stay in a more interesting IC research role vs move to a somewhat higher-paid, less interesting team-lead role”), but several large research literatures point in the same direction.

## Short version

For people who are already well-paid, **the day-to-day quality of the work**—especially how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-producing it is—tends to predict sustained job satisfaction **more strongly** than modest differences in pay.

At the same time, **leadership opportunity can matter a lot** if leading people is itself an important source of meaning or growth for you. Research would treat that not as “just status,” but as a potentially important part of person-job fit.

So the evidence is not “interesting work always wins.” It is more:

- **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once basic financial needs are well covered**
- **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work matters a lot**
- **Fit matters**: if “leading a small team” is something you deeply want, that can offset some loss of research-interest

## The main findings

### 1) Pay is only a weak-to-moderate predictor of job satisfaction

One of the most directly relevant findings is that **actual pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction**.

- A well-known meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction** found a **small positive relationship** between pay level and job satisfaction—roughly in the “weak” range rather than “strong.”  
  - Reference: **Judge et al. (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature*.

That doesn’t mean pay is unimportant. It means that after a point, **differences in pay explain surprisingly little of why people like or dislike their jobs**.

For someone in a high-compensation tech/research role, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, on average, to move well-being as much as a big change in the quality of the work itself.

---

### 2) Interesting work is not just a preference; it maps onto several well-studied drivers of well-being

What people call “interesting work” usually overlaps with several constructs that have strong evidence behind them:

- **Task variety**
- **Challenge**
- **Skill use**
- **Learning/growth**
- **Autonomy**
- **Meaning/significance**
- **Feedback/mastery**

These come from the **Job Characteristics Model** and later work-design research.

A major meta-analysis found that these work characteristics are **substantially related** to outcomes like:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

Reference:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design characteristics.

This is one of the strongest literatures relevant to your question. In plain language:  
**jobs that are more varied, meaningful, autonomous, and feedback-rich tend to make people happier and more motivated at work.**

---

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic and review evidence generally finds that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- greater life meaning / psychological well-being
- lower burnout and turnover intentions

Representative references:
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)**, review on meaningful work
- **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work on meaningful work outcomes

The important point here is that “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often travel together in research careers. If a role is “much less interesting,” it often also reduces:
- curiosity
- mastery
- flow
- identity fit

Those losses tend to matter a lot for long-run satisfaction.

---

### 4) Intrinsic motivation predicts better outcomes than extrinsic motivation

A very robust line of work from **Self-Determination Theory** finds that people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that satisfy these needs tend to produce better:
- well-being
- engagement
- persistence
- performance

References:
- **Deci & Ryan (2000)**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**
- later meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work

This matters because “interesting work” often means:
- I feel competent
- I want to think about the problems
- I’m internally pulled toward the work

That kind of intrinsic motivation is usually a better predictor of durable satisfaction than external incentives alone.

Relatedly, research on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations** generally finds that prioritizing intrinsic goals is associated with higher well-being than prioritizing extrinsic ones like money/status alone.

---

### 5) Income improves well-being, but with diminishing returns

The income-and-well-being literature is more nuanced than the old “money doesn’t matter” take.

The current best summary is something like:

- Higher income generally does improve well-being
- But the relationship is **nonlinear / diminishing**
- For already high earners, a modest increase often produces a relatively small gain compared with large changes in daily lived experience

Relevant papers:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — older plateau framing
- **Killingsworth (2021)**
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)** — more recent reconciliation showing well-being generally rises with income, but not in a simple linear way

For your case, that likely implies:

> If both jobs already put you well above financial stress, then a somewhat higher salary probably matters less for happiness than whether you enjoy spending your weekdays doing the work.

---

### 6) Person-job fit is a major moderator

This is especially relevant because your tradeoff is not just “interesting work vs money.” It is also:

- **IC research fit** vs **leadership fit**
- technical curiosity vs management aspiration

The **person-environment fit** literature consistently finds that fit between the person and the role predicts:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower turnover intention

Reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-job and person-organization fit

So if you’ve “always wanted to lead a small team,” that is not a trivial side note. It means the Anthropic role may fit an important part of your motivational profile even if the content is less interesting.

Research would predict the best outcome not from a generic rule, but from the role that better matches your deeper motives.

## Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

On **regret specifically**, I don’t know of a strong, famous literature that cleanly answers your exact question.

What the research does suggest is:

1. **People adapt fairly quickly to external improvements** like salary/status
2. They adapt less to the **daily texture of experience**—what they actually spend hours doing every day
3. Choices based on **intrinsic motivation / interest / meaning** tend to produce more durable satisfaction than choices driven mainly by external rewards

So while I wouldn’t claim “people who choose the interesting job regret it less” as a clean proven fact, the broader evidence points toward:

- **less durable benefit from moderate pay increases**
- **more durable benefit from intrinsically rewarding work**
- unless the alternative role satisfies a deeper need such as leadership, impact, or autonomy

## What the literature would probably predict in your case

Without giving a personal opinion, the research would roughly predict:

### You are more likely to be happier at Google if:
- the current work is genuinely much more interesting
- it gives you autonomy, challenge, and learning
- the Anthropic role would noticeably reduce curiosity/engagement
- the salary increase is meaningful but not transformative

### You may be happier at Anthropic if:
- leading a team is a major intrinsic goal for you, not just a résumé badge
- the role increases autonomy, scope, impact, or meaning enough to offset reduced technical interest
- you care strongly about growth into leadership, and delaying that would itself become a source of dissatisfaction

In research terms, the second case is not “choosing money over interest.” It may be “choosing a different kind of intrinsically meaningful work.”

## A research-grounded way to compare the offers

If you want to apply the literature rather than rely on intuition, score each role on these dimensions:

1. **Interest / curiosity**  
   How often will the work feel mentally engaging?

2. **Autonomy**  
   How much control will you have over problems, methods, priorities?

3. **Competence growth**  
   Which role will make you better at things you care about?

4. **Meaning / impact**  
   Does the work feel important to you?

5. **Relatedness / team quality**  
   How strong are the likely colleagues and manager?

6. **Leadership fit**  
   Is managing a team intrinsically important to you?

7. **Identity fit**  
   Which role fits who you want to become in 3–5 years?

8. **Financial impact**  
   Not “higher salary,” but: after tax, after risk, after expected adaptation, how much does it actually change your life?

If you want a single sentence summary of the evidence, it would be:

> Among already highly paid workers, sustained happiness is usually driven more by the quality and fit of the work itself than by moderate pay differences; however, a leadership role can be worth a lot if leadership is itself part of what makes work meaningful to you.

If you want, I can also turn this into a **decision memo based only on the evidence**—for example, a 1-page framework that weights the factors using what the literature suggests.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**For job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being, intrinsic job qualities — especially interesting work, autonomy, meaning, and fit with your interests — are usually stronger predictors than pay, once pay is already good.**

That does **not** mean salary and promotion don’t matter. They do. But in samples of professionals, the extra happiness from a modest pay increase is typically smaller than people expect, while the day-to-day experience of the work itself matters a lot.

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction
This is one of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology.

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), which says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

Later **meta-analyses** have generally supported this. In particular:

- **Autonomy** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Interesting, varied, significant work** also predicts higher satisfaction and lower turnover intentions.
- These effects are not tiny; they are often moderate and practically meaningful.

A particularly useful synthesis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design.  
  Broad conclusion: motivational job characteristics like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback are robustly associated with **job satisfaction, engagement, performance, and lower strain**.

### 2) “Meaningful work” is strongly associated with well-being
There is also a newer literature specifically on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- greater **work engagement**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intentions**

A useful reference:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being outcomes.  
  Broad conclusion: meaningful work has a reliable positive association with both work-related and general well-being.

This is relevant because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in practice.

### 3) Pay matters, but less than most people think
There is good evidence that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

A widely cited result:

- **Judge et al.** meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**  
  Broad conclusion: the correlation is **positive but small**.

In plain English:
- More money tends to make people somewhat more satisfied.
- But the relationship is much weaker than lay intuition suggests.
- What matters more than absolute pay is often:
  - whether pay feels **fair**
  - whether it reduces real financial stress
  - whether it signals **status/recognition**
  - whether it comes with better work conditions

This is especially important in your situation because you are already comparing two very high-paying jobs. The literature generally suggests that **at high income levels, the incremental well-being return to “a bit more salary” is relatively small** compared with differences in daily work experience.

### 4) Self-determination research: autonomy, competence, and relatedness matter a lot
**Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) is another large research program relevant here.

It finds that people thrive when work supports three basic psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice and control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

When these are satisfied at work, people report more:
- well-being
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- persistence

And less:
- burnout
- strain
- disengagement

Useful references:
- **Deci & Ryan (2000)**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**
- **Van den Broeck et al.** meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work

This literature often ends up favoring “interesting work” because interesting work usually supports **competence and intrinsic motivation**, and often autonomy too.

### 5) Person-job fit matters a lot
A very robust finding is that **fit** matters.

People tend to do better when their job matches:
- their interests
- values
- strengths
- preferred style of working

If someone values technical depth and discovery, a move into a role with more coordination, politics, and people management can reduce satisfaction even if it is objectively “better” on paper.

Conversely, if someone has a strong desire to **lead**, mentor, and shape a team, then management can be intrinsically rewarding — not just a status move.

So in research terms, your choice is not simply:
- “interesting work” vs “more money”

It is more like:
- **technical interest/content**
vs
- **leadership aspiration/role fit**
plus
- some salary difference

That distinction matters.

### 6) Promotions and management roles: often mixed
The research on promotions/management is more mixed than people assume.

Typical findings:
- Promotions often increase **status**, pay, and sense of progress.
- They can increase **job satisfaction**, especially in the short term.
- But they also often increase **stress**, working hours, role ambiguity, and responsibility.
- They do **not** reliably improve overall well-being for everyone.

So “leading a small team” is not automatically better or worse. It tends to be better **if the person actually wants the leadership work itself**, not just the title.

This is one of the key things your question highlights: you say leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.” That matters, because from a research standpoint that makes leadership a potential **intrinsic reward**, not merely an extrinsic one.

### 7) What about regret?
This specific question — “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the better-paid/promoted one?” — is **less directly studied** than job satisfaction.

There is not, to my knowledge, a large clean literature on this exact tradeoff framed as “regret.”

But adjacent literatures suggest:

- People who prioritize **intrinsic aspirations** (growth, meaning, relationships, contribution) tend to have **better well-being** than people who prioritize **extrinsic aspirations** (money, image, status) too heavily.
- People often **overestimate** how much extra money or status will improve their lived experience.
- Day-to-day quality of work is a repeated experience, so it has a large cumulative effect on well-being.

Relevant work here comes from:
- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
- general work on **affective forecasting** and misprediction of future happiness

So while “regret” data are thinner, the broader evidence points toward:
- choosing work that is more interesting/meaningful/fitting usually does **not** look like a mistake in well-being terms, especially when the salary difference is modest and both options are financially excellent.

## The strongest overall conclusion
If I synthesize the literature as conservatively as possible:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but its effect is usually smaller than people expect once compensation is already high.**
3. **Leadership can improve satisfaction if it matches your motives; otherwise it often raises stress without improving well-being much.**
4. **Fit matters more than prestige.** A role that matches what you actually enjoy tends to produce better outcomes.

## What this means for your exact tradeoff
From a research perspective, the key question is not just:

- “Google work is more interesting”
vs
- “Anthropic pays more”

It is:

- How much do you value **day-to-day intellectual interest**?
- How much do you value **leading people as an activity**, not just as a milestone?
- How much autonomy would each role have?
- Which role better fits your actual motivational profile?

If leading a team is genuinely intrinsically meaningful to you, then the Anthropic role may still score well on the same dimensions the research says matter.

If, however, the daily work there would feel substantially less engaging and the salary bump is only modest, the literature would generally predict that **staying with the more interesting work is more likely to maximize satisfaction**.

## If you want the short answer
**Yes: people in more interesting/meaningful/autonomous jobs are usually happier with their work, and the evidence for salary alone compensating for boring work is weak once pay is already high.**  
The main exception is when the “less interesting” job is a much better **fit** because it satisfies another strong intrinsic motive — in your case, a real desire to lead.

## A few concrete references to start with
If you want papers/books to actually read, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000)** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005)** — SDT applied to work motivation
- **Van den Broeck et al.** — meta-analytic work on psychological need satisfaction at work
- **Judge et al.** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay** — meaningful work and well-being
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you a **more academic answer with 10–15 specific papers and one-sentence summaries of each**, focused exactly on:
- interesting work vs pay,
- management/promotion vs individual contributor roles,
- and long-term well-being/regret.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad result is fairly consistent:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-producing the work feels — tend to predict **job satisfaction and wellbeing more strongly** than **pay differences**, especially once pay is already high enough that money is not changing basic security.

A research-based summary:

## 1) Interesting work usually matters a lot

### Job design research
A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** by **Hackman & Oldham (1976)**. The core idea is that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they offer:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are very close to what people often mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007, Journal of Applied Psychology)** found that these kinds of job characteristics are associated with higher:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal / turnover intentions

Importantly, these relationships were not tiny. They were generally **moderate in size**, and typically stronger than what you see for pay alone.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to wellbeing

A newer literature focuses on **meaningful work** rather than just “interestingness.”

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. (2019)** found that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- better overall wellbeing / life satisfaction
- lower distress / negative outcomes

Again, the pattern is pretty robust: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to do better psychologically.

That does **not** prove that interesting work always causes happiness — some of this is correlational — but across studies the association is strong enough that it would be odd to ignore it.

## 3) Autonomy and mastery matter a lot

Another very relevant framework is **Self-Determination Theory**. Across many studies, people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have control
- **competence** — feeling effective / able to grow
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Meta-analytic reviews in this tradition consistently find that satisfying these needs at work predicts:

- more motivation
- greater engagement
- greater wellbeing
- less burnout

This is important because “interesting work” often bundles together **autonomy + challenge + mastery**, which is a powerful combination.

## 4) Pay matters — but less than many people expect

The key paper here is **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

Their basic result: **the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is positive but modest**. In plain English:

- more pay helps
- but it is **far from the dominant driver** of job satisfaction

This is especially true when comparing two already well-paid jobs. A **small-to-moderate salary increase** usually does **not** compensate much for a daily experience that is less engaging or meaningful.

Related work on income and wellbeing also suggests **diminishing returns**: money matters a lot when it changes security and stress, but much less once you’re already in a comfortable range.

So if the choice is roughly:

- **much more interesting work**
vs
- **somewhat higher salary**

the literature usually leans toward the first for day-to-day wellbeing.

## 5) Leadership is different: it can help, but mostly if it fits you

This part is more nuanced.

There is strong research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** — for example **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**. The consistent finding is that fit predicts:

- higher job satisfaction
- better commitment
- lower turnover intentions

So the fact that **leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted** matters a lot. That is not just an “extrinsic perk.” It may reflect a real **values/identity fit**.

In other words:

- If leadership is intrinsically important to you, then the Anthropic role may be more satisfying than “less interesting work” alone would suggest.
- If you mainly love research and only abstractly like the idea of leadership, then moving into management can backfire, because management often means less of the intellectually absorbing work you currently enjoy.

Research on promotions and management transitions generally finds mixed effects:
- some boost in status / satisfaction
- but also more stress, role conflict, meetings, coordination load, and responsibility

So management is **not** universally a happiness upgrade. It depends heavily on fit.

## 6) What about regret?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

There is much more research on:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- wellbeing

than on explicit long-term **career regret**.

Still, the indirect evidence suggests:
- choices aligned with **intrinsic motives** and **self-concordant goals** tend to produce better longer-term wellbeing
- chasing mostly extrinsic rewards tends to be less satisfying than people predict

So while I can’t point to a very strong “people regret choosing interesting work vs salary” literature specifically, the broader evidence suggests that people do better when their work aligns with what they find genuinely engaging and personally important.

## A cautious bottom line from the research

If I translate the literature into your situation:

### The evidence generally favors staying with the more interesting work when:
- the salary difference is not life-changing
- both jobs are already financially excellent
- the current role gives strong intellectual engagement / mastery
- the new role is materially less interesting day to day

### The evidence favors taking the new role when:
- leading people is itself a deeply meaningful goal for you
- the new role offers stronger autonomy, growth, and fit with your long-term identity
- the leadership opportunity is unusually good and hard to replicate later

## If you want the most research-grounded way to frame the decision

The literature suggests you should not think in terms of **“interesting work” vs “salary”** only.

A better research-based decomposition is:

1. **Intrinsic work quality**  
   How engaging, meaningful, challenging, and autonomy-supportive is the day-to-day work?

2. **Fit with your motives**  
   Do you actually want to be a manager, or do you want the option / prestige of it?

3. **Social environment**  
   Who would you work with? Research consistently shows manager/team quality matters a lot.

4. **Workload / stress**  
   Leadership roles often increase cognitive fragmentation and responsibility load.

5. **Compensation**  
   Important, but usually weaker than the factors above once pay is already high.

If you want, I can give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses** on this topic, or turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the literature** rather than personal advice.

answer:
Yes—there is a fair amount of research on this, though not usually framed as exactly “interesting work vs. a somewhat higher salary and a team-lead role.” The closest literatures are:

- **job design / intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **self-determination theory at work**
- **pay vs. job satisfaction**
- **person-job fit**
- and, more loosely, **intrinsic vs. extrinsic aspirations**

## Short version

**On average, people are happier in jobs they find intrinsically engaging/meaningful/autonomous than in jobs that simply pay more.**  
The evidence for that is fairly strong.

A useful summary from the literature would be:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work and autonomy** are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work engagement.
2. **Pay matters**, but its relationship with job satisfaction is **positive and surprisingly modest**, especially once pay is already high enough that money is not solving major life constraints.
3. **Leadership/promotion** is more mixed: it can improve satisfaction if it brings autonomy, growth, and identity fit, but it can also increase stress and reduce day-to-day enjoyment if the actual work is less aligned with what you like doing.
4. There is **less direct research on “regret”** specifically, but the literature on intrinsic vs. extrinsic goals suggests that choosing mainly for money/status tends to produce **less durable well-being** than choosing for fit, meaning, autonomy, and mastery.

## The most relevant findings

### 1) Interesting work is strongly tied to job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses of **work design**.

The key job features are things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are basically the building blocks of “interesting work” in org-psych terms.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**

found that these job characteristics are strongly associated with outcomes like:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **meaningfulness**
- **engagement**
- lower strain/withdrawal in many cases

The broad takeaway is very consistent: **jobs that feel interesting, meaningful, and self-directed tend to be substantially better for subjective work well-being.**

### 2) Meaningful work has a strong association with happiness-related outcomes

Another direct literature is on **meaningful work**.

A meta-analysis by:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**

found that perceived meaningful work is positively related to:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **organizational commitment**

and negatively related to:

- **turnover intentions**
- **cynicism / burnout-like outcomes**

This is important for your question because “interesting” work often overlaps with “meaningful” work, though they are not identical. The evidence suggests that if your current role feels genuinely more interesting and intellectually alive, that is not a trivial preference—it is one of the job attributes most reliably linked to well-being.

### 3) Pay is much less predictive of job satisfaction than people often expect

One of the cleanest findings in this area:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**

The headline result is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**—around **r ≈ .15**.

That is a very useful number. In plain English:

- higher pay does increase satisfaction **a bit**
- but it explains only a **small fraction** of why people like or dislike their jobs

So if the question is “does slightly more money usually compensate for less interesting work?” the research answer is roughly:

**usually not, unless the pay difference is large enough to materially change life conditions, reduce financial stress, or buy something you care deeply about.**

That is even more likely to be true when both options are already high-paying jobs.

### 4) At work, autonomy/competence/relatedness matter a lot

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory**.

Across many studies, when work satisfies the needs for:

- **autonomy** — I have choice/control
- **competence** — I’m developing mastery / doing things well
- **relatedness** — I feel connected to people I work with

people tend to report better:

- well-being
- engagement
- motivation
- persistence
- lower burnout

Meta-analytic reviews of basic psychological needs at work consistently support this pattern.

This matters because a team-lead role may reduce one kind of interest (technical/research interest) while increasing another set of satisfiers:

- more autonomy
- more responsibility
- more mentoring
- stronger sense of impact

So the literature would not say “interesting technical work always wins.” It would say:

> The role that best supports autonomy, competence, relatedness, and meaningfulness is likely to produce more satisfaction.

### 5) Fit matters: people are happier when the job matches their interests and values

There is also a strong literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

found that better fit is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower turnover intentions

So if Google is more aligned with your curiosity and Anthropic is more aligned with your leadership aspiration, the research question becomes:

**Which role fits your deeper motivations better?**

Not:
- “Which has the higher salary?”
- or even just “Which sounds more prestigious?”

### 6) Intrinsic motives generally predict better well-being than extrinsic ones

A related line of research, especially from **Kasser & Ryan** and later self-determination work, distinguishes:

- **intrinsic goals**: growth, relationships, community, mastery, meaning
- **extrinsic goals**: money, status, image

Across many studies, stronger emphasis on **extrinsic rewards alone** is associated with **lower well-being**, while intrinsic goal orientation is associated with better well-being.

This is not saying money/status are bad. It is saying:

> When decisions are driven mainly by extrinsic factors, they tend to be less satisfying over time than decisions that also satisfy intrinsic needs.

That is probably the closest research analogue to your “will I regret it later?” question.

## What about regret specifically?

There is **much less direct evidence** on “people who chose the more interesting job later regretted it” versus “people who chose the higher-paying less-interesting job regretted it.”

What the literature gives you instead are proxies:

- **job satisfaction**
- **engagement**
- **burnout**
- **turnover intentions**
- **mental well-being**
- **value congruence**

Those proxies generally favor:

- meaningful work
- autonomy
- skill use
- fit with interests/values

over small differences in pay.

If you want the closest conclusion the literature supports, it would be:

> People more often suffer from a chronic, recurring dissatisfaction when daily work is uninteresting/misaligned than from not taking a modestly higher-paying option.

That’s not a direct “regret study,” but it is the pattern implied by the evidence base.

## Important nuance for your exact case

Your tradeoff is not just:

- **interesting work** vs.
- **more money**

It is also:

- **IC/research role** vs.
- **leadership role**

And leadership could increase some of the things the literature says matter:

- autonomy
- sense of impact
- identity fit
- competence growth in a new domain
- relatedness/mentoring

So the literature does **not** imply that staying at Google is automatically better for happiness.

A more research-faithful way to frame your case would be:

### Google may score higher on:
- intellectual interest
- intrinsic enjoyment
- curiosity
- possibly mastery in your strongest domain

### Anthropic may score higher on:
- leadership aspiration fulfillment
- autonomy/status
- role expansion
- impact through others
- maybe compensation

Research would predict better well-being from the option that wins on the **bundle** of:
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- competence growth
- values fit
- social quality
- manageable demands

—not just whichever is “more interesting” in a narrow technical sense.

## One practical evidence-based inference

Because you are already comparing two elite, high-paying roles, the literature would generally predict:

- the **incremental well-being gain from “a bit more salary” is likely small**
- the effect of **daily work content and job design** is likely larger
- but if **leading a team is a deeply held aspiration**, that may itself be an intrinsic source of meaning and growth, not merely an extrinsic reward

So the research doesn’t say “always choose the interesting job.”  
It says something closer to:

> Choose the role whose day-to-day activity best satisfies your intrinsic motives and psychological needs, because those are more strongly linked to long-run satisfaction than modest pay differences.

## If you want the best papers to read

I’d start with these:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   Meta-analysis on work design and job characteristics.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

3. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   Meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes.

4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit.

5. Reviews/meta-analyses on **Self-Determination Theory at work**  
   especially need satisfaction (autonomy, competence, relatedness) and well-being.

6. **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs. extrinsic aspirations  
   for the broader question of what kinds of goals predict durable well-being.

If you want, I can give you a **more systematic literature summary** with:
- the paper names,
- the main findings,
- rough effect sizes,
- and which ones are strongest for a decision like yours.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, and the broad pattern is pretty consistent:

## Short version

If pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the main issue, **intrinsic job quality** — interesting work, autonomy, learning, meaning, fit, good colleagues — tends to predict **job satisfaction and sustained well-being more strongly** than a modest increase in salary.

So, **on average**, people in more interesting / intrinsically rewarding jobs do tend to report higher job satisfaction and often better overall well-being than people in less interesting jobs with somewhat better extrinsic rewards.

The big caveat in your case is: **leading a team is not just an extrinsic perk**. If you genuinely want to lead, that is itself an intrinsic motivator for some people. So your tradeoff is not simply “interesting work vs money”; it is more like **technical interest vs leadership aspiration**.

---

## What the research generally says

### 1. Interesting work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A large body of organizational psychology research finds that work is more satisfying when it has:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for mastery/learning**

This comes from the classic **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses.

A widely cited meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**: work design characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, and significance showed **strong relationships** with job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover intentions.

In practical terms:  
If your current job is “very interesting,” that is not a soft or trivial factor. In the literature, it is one of the core drivers of satisfaction.

---

### 2. Pay matters, but less than many people expect once compensation is already good
There is also a lot of evidence that:

- higher pay is associated with higher satisfaction,
- **but the relationship is only modest**,
- and **relative pay / fairness** often matters more than absolute pay.

A relevant meta-analysis:
- **Judge et al.** on pay and job satisfaction: the association is **positive but not large**. In other words, salary helps, but it is not usually the dominant driver of whether people like their jobs.

This is especially relevant at companies like Google and Anthropic, where compensation is already likely in the “high enough that basic needs are not the issue” range. In that range, research usually finds that **differences in day-to-day work experience matter more** than a somewhat higher paycheck.

---

### 3. Meaningful or engaging work has robust links to well-being
Another strong line of research is on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work here finds that perceived meaningfulness is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower turnover intention
- better general well-being

A useful reference:
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis on meaningful work

“Interesting” is not identical to “meaningful,” but in real jobs they often overlap: intellectually engaging work tends to support a sense of competence, growth, and purpose.

---

### 4. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness are central
A very influential framework in motivation research is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan).

It says people function best when work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected and valued

Interesting technical work often scores high on competence and sometimes autonomy.  
Leading a small team may increase autonomy/relatedness/impact, but reduce hands-on competence-building if the work itself is less stimulating.

This is important because it suggests there is no single “intrinsic vs extrinsic” axis. Different jobs satisfy different psychological needs.

---

### 5. Person-job fit matters a lot
Another robust result: **fit** matters.

If a role matches your values, identity, and strengths, you tend to be more satisfied and stay longer. This comes from research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

A classic meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**

This matters for your specific case because:
- if you identify strongly as a researcher who wants deep technical novelty, Google may fit better;
- if you deeply want to build and lead people, the Anthropic role may fit better.

Research would predict that **the better-fitting role tends to produce higher long-run satisfaction**, even if the “objective” prestige/pay comparison points the other way.

---

## What about regret?

This is where the evidence is thinner.

I don’t know of a strong, famous body of research that directly asks:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership one?”

What we do have are nearby findings:

### A. People often overweight extrinsic markers when forecasting happiness
Behavioral research on **affective forecasting** suggests people often mispredict what will make them happy, and often overweight salient external factors like pay, title, or prestige relative to day-to-day experience.

That implies a common mistake is:
- focusing on salary/title at decision time,
- while underweighting how enjoyable the actual work will feel every day.

### B. Adaptation often weakens the impact of raises/promotions
There is evidence in happiness economics that people **adapt** to income gains and status gains. The boost is real, but often not as lasting as expected.

There is also research suggesting promotions can increase satisfaction, but not always durably — especially if they bring more stress, less autonomy, or poorer fit.

So if by “regret” you mean “later wishing I had optimized for day-to-day experience instead of salary/title,” the literature gives that concern some support.

But I would be careful claiming a clean causal result like:
- “people who choose interesting work regret it less.”

That exact claim is stronger than the evidence I can honestly support.

---

## A subtle but important point for your case

Your dilemma is not really just:

- **interesting work** vs **more money**

It is at least three things:

1. **interesting research content**
2. **leadership opportunity**
3. **compensation difference**

Research strongly suggests #1 matters a lot.  
Research also suggests #3 matters, but less than many people think once pay is already high.  
And #2 can matter a lot **if leading is genuinely something you want**, because it can satisfy autonomy, impact, identity, and growth.

So from a research perspective, the key question becomes:

> Is “leading a small team” intrinsically meaningful to you, or mostly attractive as a milestone/status marker?

If it is intrinsically meaningful, then the Anthropic role may still score well on the same psychological variables that predict satisfaction.

If it is mostly symbolic, and the actual work is “much less interesting,” then the literature would lean toward expecting lower long-term satisfaction despite the higher salary and title.

---

## If I had to summarize the evidence in one sentence

**For people already earning plenty, long-run satisfaction tends to be driven more by the quality and fit of the daily work than by modest pay increases — but leadership opportunities can be highly satisfying when they match a real personal motive rather than an abstract career ideal.**

---

## Useful references / lines of research to look up

If you want papers to read, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design and outcomes
- **Deci & Ryan** / **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory at work
- **Judge et al.** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)** — person-job/person-organization fit meta-analysis
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meaningful work meta-analysis
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and well-being, with nuance about diminishing returns and ongoing gains

---

## Bottom line from the literature

If both jobs already pay very well, the research generally points to:

- **interesting / autonomous / meaningful / growth-oriented work** being a stronger predictor of happiness than a small salary bump;
- **fit** being crucial;
- and **leadership** being a positive only if you genuinely want the day-to-day reality of managing, not just the idea of having managed.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the research** — e.g. a 6-factor scorecard based on what best predicts long-run job satisfaction.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, and the short version is:

**Across studies, “interesting / meaningful / autonomous” work tends to predict sustained job satisfaction and wellbeing more strongly than incremental pay does, especially once pay is already high enough.**  
But the crucial caveat is that **leadership itself can be an intrinsic reward** if it fits your values and identity. So your choice is not simply “interesting work vs money”; it may be “technical interest vs leadership/impact.”

A few research-backed points:

## 1) Interesting work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction
A large body of organizational psychology research finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for mastery / learning**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses have generally supported it.

One major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed work-design research and found that characteristics like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback had **moderate-to-strong relationships** with outcomes such as:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - lower strain / burnout

So if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, challenging, and growth-producing, that is very much in the set of things the literature says matter.

## 2) Meaningful work has one of the strongest links to wellbeing
Another strong result is that **meaningful work** is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- less distress / burnout

A useful summary is:

- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis on meaningful work

The effect sizes there are not tiny; by social-science standards, they are substantial. Meaningfulness at work is one of the more reliable predictors of positive work outcomes.

If the Google role feels genuinely more meaningful or intellectually alive, that likely matters a lot.

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people think for long-run satisfaction
Research on **pay and job satisfaction** usually finds a **positive but fairly small** relationship.

Across meta-analyses, the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is usually in the **small range** — roughly much smaller than the effects of things like:
- autonomy
- fit
- interestingness / meaningfulness
- relationship with manager
- fairness

Important nuance:
- If pay is **too low**, or feels **unfair**, it matters a lot.
- But once someone is already well-compensated, **additional money tends to have diminishing returns** for day-to-day wellbeing.

That does **not** mean “money doesn’t matter.”  
It means that in decisions among already-excellent options, **job content often matters more than salary delta** for long-term satisfaction.

## 4) Intrinsic motives usually beat extrinsic motives for wellbeing
This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and related work.

The most replicated finding is that people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And people tend to have higher wellbeing when their choices are guided more by **intrinsic goals** (growth, mastery, contribution, connection) than primarily by **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image).

Classic work by **Kasser & Ryan** found that prioritizing extrinsic goals is generally associated with lower wellbeing than prioritizing intrinsic goals.

Applied to your case:
- “The salary is a bit higher” is an extrinsic gain.
- “I always wanted to lead a small team” may actually be **intrinsic**, if it reflects identity, mastery, mentorship, or desire for impact.
- “The work is much less interesting” is a loss on a dimension the research says matters a lot.

So the research would tell you not to reduce this to salary. It would ask: **which role better satisfies your intrinsic motives?**

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot
A large meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** found that various kinds of fit — especially person-job and person-organization fit — are strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower turnover intentions

This matters because “leading a small team” is not universally good or bad. It depends on fit.

If you genuinely have a strong leadership motive, and the Anthropic role fits that, then the leadership component may add a lot to your satisfaction even if the subject matter is less interesting.

If, on the other hand, you mainly like the *idea* of leadership but would actually miss technical depth, creation, and discovery, then the fit may be worse than it looks.

## 6) Regret is studied less directly than satisfaction, but the pattern points the same way
There isn’t, as far as I know, a clean famous literature of the form:

> “People who chose more interesting jobs later regretted it less.”

Most of the evidence is on:
- job satisfaction
- wellbeing
- burnout
- engagement
- turnover

But the indirect conclusion is fairly consistent:

People tend to do worse when they move into roles with poorer fit, less intrinsic motivation, less meaning, and less autonomy — even when the role brings more pay or status.

In other words, the literature is stronger on **“what predicts wellbeing”** than on **“what people later report as regret.”**  
Still, those predictors usually point in the same direction.

## 7) Management specifically is mixed: it helps if you actually want it
There’s also a separate literature suggesting that being promoted into management is often not automatically beneficial.

People are frequently moved into management because they were excellent individual contributors, not because they have:
- motivation for management
- aptitude for coaching
- enjoyment of coordination / people work
- tolerance for reduced technical depth

That doesn’t mean management is bad. It means **management is satisfying mostly when it fits the person**.

For someone who has “always wanted to lead a small team,” that could be very important.  
But it is worth noting that leadership often comes with:
- less time on the intellectually interesting core work
- more coordination, hiring, performance management, conflict resolution
- more context-switching
- more responsibility for others’ output rather than your own

Research would say: if those things are meaningful to you, great. If they mainly crowd out the work you love, not great.

---

# So what does the research suggest in your exact tradeoff?

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

**For long-run happiness, the role that better delivers intrinsic motivation, meaning, autonomy, and fit is usually the better bet than the one that merely pays somewhat more.**

In your description, Google seems to win on:
- interest
- likely mastery / flow
- maybe meaning

Anthropic seems to win on:
- salary
- leadership opportunity
- perhaps scope / responsibility

The key research-based question is:

## Is “leading a small team” for you an intrinsic value on par with “doing interesting research”?
Because if yes, then Anthropic is not “less interesting but more money”; it’s more like:

- less intellectually interesting
- more leadership-fit / responsibility / mentoring / impact

And the literature would say that could absolutely be worth it **if that role satisfies your deeper motives better**.

---

# If you want the most evidence-based way to think about it
Rate each role from 1–10 on these dimensions:

1. **Autonomy**
2. **Competence / learning / challenge**
3. **Meaning / significance**
4. **Daily interest**
5. **Leadership fit**
6. **Relationships / culture fit**
7. **Fairness of compensation**
8. **Expected stress / burnout risk**
9. **Future option value**
10. **Identity fit** (“Does this feel like the kind of person I want to become?”)

Research suggests long-run satisfaction tracks those dimensions better than salary alone.

---

# A cautious bottom line
If both jobs already clear the “excellent compensation” threshold, then:

- **Interesting, meaningful work usually predicts more sustained satisfaction than extra pay.**
- **Leadership can be just as important as interesting technical content if it is deeply identity-congruent.**
- **The strongest predictor is probably not salary, but fit.**

So the research-based answer is not “always stay with the more interesting job.”  
It is:

> **Choose the option with the stronger bundle of intrinsic rewards and better person-role fit.**

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **short annotated reading list** of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **decision framework grounded in this literature** that you can actually use on your Google vs Anthropic choice.

answer:
Yes. The research base is not on the exact choice “Google research role vs Anthropic team lead,” but there is a lot on the underlying tradeoff:

- **intrinsic job qualities**: interesting/challenging work, autonomy, learning, meaning
- **extrinsic job qualities**: pay, title, status
- **fit**: whether the role matches what *you* value, including a desire to lead

The broad result is fairly consistent:

> **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being. Pay matters too, but usually less than people expect once income is already high enough for comfort/security.**

A few concrete research lines:

## 1) “Interesting work” strongly predicts job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied indirectly via **job characteristics** like:

- skill variety
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback
- problem-solving / complexity

The classic model is **Hackman & Oldham**; the big meta-analytic update is **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

Their conclusion, in plain English: jobs that are richer in autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback are associated with substantially higher:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- performance
- lower turnover intentions

This is one of the most replicated findings in work psychology.

### Practical interpretation
If your current role is genuinely more intellectually engaging and stimulating on a daily basis, research would predict that this matters a lot for how good the job feels.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic incentives for sustained satisfaction

A second large literature is **Self-Determination Theory** (**Deci & Ryan**; workplace reviews/meta-analyses by **Gagné**, **Van den Broeck**, others).

The core finding: people do better and feel better at work when the job supports:

- **autonomy** — I have meaningful control
- **competence** — I’m learning, mastering, growing
- **relatedness** — I’m connected to others / contributing

Work that is interesting or absorbing usually supports autonomy + competence especially well.

This literature repeatedly finds that **need satisfaction at work predicts engagement, satisfaction, and lower burnout**. Purely extrinsic rewards like money/status help much less if the work itself is deadening.

### Important nuance for your case
A leadership role can also satisfy those same needs:
- autonomy
- competence in a new domain
- relatedness via mentorship/team building

So “less interesting” is not automatically worse if part of the interest is shifting from technical content to people leadership.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less than job quality once basic needs are covered

The key meta-analysis here is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on **pay and job satisfaction**.

Their result is often summarized as:

- the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**
- it is much smaller than many people intuitively assume

So higher pay does make people somewhat more satisfied, but not by a lot.

Related well-being research in economics/psychology:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: income improves life evaluation and emotional well-being, with diminishing returns
- **Killingsworth (2021/2023)**: well-being continues to rise at higher incomes more than Kahneman/Deaton suggested, but still with strong individual differences and generally diminishing marginal impact

### The relevant takeaway
For already well-paid professionals, **an incremental salary bump often has less effect on happiness than changing the actual texture of everyday work**.

That does **not** mean money doesn’t matter:
- it matters a lot if it changes security, freedom, family options, or perceived fairness
- it matters less as a compensator for boring work once you’re already financially comfortable

---

## 4) Meaningful work and person-job fit predict well-being and retention

A related literature studies **meaningful work** and **person-job fit**.

### Person-job fit
The major meta-analysis is **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**.

People are more satisfied and less likely to want to quit when their jobs fit:
- their abilities
- their values
- their needs
- their preferences

This is especially relevant because your tradeoff is not just “interest vs salary.” It is also:

- research depth vs managerial breadth
- technical craft vs leading people
- current identity vs future identity

If you’ve “always wanted to lead a small team,” that leadership element is not just an external reward. It may represent **better fit** with your long-term motives.

### Meaningful work
Meta-analytic and review work by **Allan** and others shows that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher life meaning
- better mental well-being
- lower turnover intention

Interesting work and meaningful work are not identical, but they often travel together.

---

## 5) On regret specifically: direct evidence is thinner, but intrinsic-fit choices generally age better

There is much less clean research on “which job choice do people later regret more?”

But the closest bodies of research suggest:

### a) Self-concordance research
Work by **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)** and related research suggests that goals aligned with one’s deeper interests/values tend to produce:
- more persistent effort
- greater satisfaction when achieved
- less ambivalence

By contrast, choices driven mainly by extrinsic motives like status or money tend to produce weaker gains in lasting well-being.

### b) Intrinsic vs extrinsic values research
Research by **Kasser & Ryan** and others repeatedly finds that people whose lives are organized more around intrinsic aims (growth, relationships, contribution) tend to show better well-being than people focused primarily on extrinsic aims (money, image, status).

That’s not a direct job-choice regret study, but it points in the same direction:
- **choosing primarily for money/title is not usually the path to the best long-term subjective outcome**
- **choosing for work that fits your interests/values usually predicts better satisfaction**

### c) Caveat
Regret is highly individualized. One common source of regret is not “I chose the higher salary,” but “I never tested whether I actually wanted the leadership path.”

So the literature would not say “always stay in the more interesting technical role.” It would say something closer to:

> People do best when their work aligns with their intrinsic motives.  
> For some people that means deep technical research; for others it means building and leading.

---

## What seems most solid from the evidence

If I compress the literature into a few research-backed points:

1. **Interesting/challenging/autonomous work has a strong relationship with job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a positive but smaller relationship with job satisfaction than many assume.**
3. **At higher income levels, extra money usually has diminishing returns for happiness.**
4. **Fit matters a lot** — if leading a team is deeply important to you, that can outweigh lower topical interest.
5. **There is little evidence that chasing salary/status alone is the best route to long-term happiness.**
6. **There is also little evidence that “more interesting content” always wins if it blocks a self-concordant growth path you genuinely want**, such as leadership.

---

## So, to your literal question: “Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?”

**On average: yes, if “interesting” means intrinsically motivating, engaging, autonomous work.**  
That is one of the better-supported findings in work psychology.

But the stronger statement is:

> People tend to be happier in jobs with stronger intrinsic rewards and better personal fit than in jobs chosen mainly for extrinsic rewards.

In your case, the leadership component may itself be an intrinsic reward, not just a résumé line.

---

## The most relevant studies to look up

If you want the shortest reading list with high signal:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features*  
   Meta-analysis on job characteristics and outcomes.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
   Best direct source on salary vs satisfaction.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis on **person-job/person-organization fit**.

4. **Gagné & Deci (2005)** and later SDT workplace reviews  
   On autonomy, competence, relatedness, and work motivation.

5. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021/2023)**  
   On income and well-being.

6. **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)**  
   On **self-concordant goals** and well-being.

7. **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work on **meaningful work**  
   For the meaning/well-being link.

---

If you want, I can do a second pass that is even more specific to **“interesting IC research role vs first-line management role”** and summarize what the evidence says about transitions from expert tracks into management.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is pretty consistent on one broad point:

**The day-to-day content of the work — whether it is interesting, challenging, autonomous, meaningful, and a good fit for your interests — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being.**  
**Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already good.**  
**Leadership/promotion can help, but mostly when it fits what the person actually wants.**

A few important caveats first:

- There is **not** much research that exactly asks: “Do people later regret choosing a more interesting IC/research role over a somewhat better-paid team-lead role?”
- The evidence is mostly **indirect**, from occupational psychology, labor economics, and well-being research.
- “Interesting work” is usually studied under related constructs like:
  - **intrinsic motivation**
  - **job characteristics** (variety, significance, autonomy, feedback)
  - **meaningful work**
  - **person-job fit / interest congruence**

## 1) “Interesting work” is usually a strong predictor of satisfaction

### Job characteristics research
The classic line of work is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris (1987)** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

The recurring finding is that jobs tend to be more satisfying and motivating when they have more:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

For knowledge workers, that cluster maps pretty well onto “the work itself is interesting.”

**Takeaway:** jobs that are richer in intrinsic content are, on average, associated with substantially higher job satisfaction and internal motivation.

## 2) Pay matters, but its direct link to job satisfaction is modest

A widely cited meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction.

Their bottom-line result is often summarized as:

- the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**

Roughly: people like being paid more, but pay alone explains surprisingly little of how satisfied they are with the job.

That does **not** mean salary is irrelevant. It means that after compensation is already “good enough,” differences in pay tend to matter less than differences in:

- the work itself
- autonomy
- fit
- manager quality
- growth
- meaning

For someone already at a high-paying company, this is especially relevant, because the marginal happiness gain from “somewhat higher salary” is often smaller than people expect.

## 3) Person-job fit matters a lot

A different but related literature is **person-environment fit** and **interest congruence**.

Representative meta-analytic work includes:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person-job / person-organization fit
- **Nye et al. (2012)** on interest congruence

The general result is:

- when the work fits your interests, values, and preferred way of working, people report:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - stronger commitment
  - lower turnover intention

This matters for your case because your tradeoff is not just “interesting vs salary.”  
It is also:

- **deep research work that fits your current interests**
vs
- **leadership responsibility that fits a long-standing aspiration**

Research would say both are real sources of satisfaction. The key question is which is more central to your own fit.

## 4) Meaningful work is strongly tied to well-being

There is a large literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic and review work (for example by **Allan**, **Steger**, and others) generally finds that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower burnout
- better overall well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in practice they often travel together, especially in research roles.

So if the Google role is not just technically stimulating but actually feels more significant and absorbing, research would generally predict that to matter a lot for happiness.

## 5) Autonomy is one of the strongest job features

Across several literatures, **autonomy** keeps showing up as a major driver of well-being.

This appears in:

- job design research
- **self-determination theory** at work
- occupational well-being studies

The basic pattern is:

- people do better when work supports
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**

That is associated with higher motivation and well-being, and lower burnout.

This can point in either direction for your decision:

- if the research role at Google gives you more intellectual autonomy, that’s a big plus
- if leading a small team at Anthropic gives you more real control, influence, and ownership, that is also a big plus

## 6) Leadership/promotion is not uniformly better

This part is often missed.

Research on promotion and management roles tends to show **mixed effects**:

- some people become more satisfied because they gain
  - status
  - influence
  - pay
  - autonomy
  - growth
- others become less satisfied because they lose
  - hands-on work they actually enjoy
  - technical depth
  - flow
  - time for the work that gave them energy

Management often also brings:

- more stress
- more coordination work
- more people problems
- more role conflict

So the literature does **not** support a simple “leading a team is better.”  
It supports: **leading a team is better when that role fits your motives and strengths.**

That is especially important for researchers/engineers, because many highly capable technical people are happier as deep individual contributors than as managers.

## 7) People often overvalue salary/status and undervalue daily experience

This is less direct, but relevant.

Work by **Kahneman**, **Krueger**, **Gilbert**, **Wilson**, and others on well-being and affective forecasting suggests that people often mispredict what will make them happier. Two recurring biases:

- over-weighting salient things like pay, prestige, or title
- under-weighting the quality of everyday experience

In career choices, this implies people may underappreciate how much it matters to spend many hours each day doing work that is genuinely engaging.

So if you are trying to predict future happiness, research suggests you should pay serious attention to:
- “What will my average Tuesday feel like?”
not just:
- “Which option sounds more impressive?”

## 8) What about regret specifically?

Direct evidence on “career regret after choosing more interesting work over more pay/leadership” is thinner.

But the closest robust findings are:

1. **Intrinsic job quality predicts more durable satisfaction** than extrinsic rewards alone.
2. People often **adapt** to pay and title faster than to the daily felt quality of the work.
3. Poor fit between person and role tends to predict later dissatisfaction and turnover.

So if you want the closest evidence-based answer, it is probably:

> People do not seem to reliably become happier just because a job pays more or carries a leadership title.  
> They are more likely to be satisfied when the work is intrinsically engaging and the role fits their motives.

## The most relevant research-backed summary for your exact tradeoff

You seem to be weighing three different predictors of satisfaction:

1. **Interesting/challenging work**  
   Strong predictor of satisfaction and engagement.

2. **Higher salary**  
   Positive, but usually weaker predictor once you’re already well compensated.

3. **Leading a small team / growth into leadership**  
   Potentially a strong positive if it reflects a real personal aspiration and good fit; potentially negative if it mainly removes you from the work you love.

So the literature would not say “always stay with the more interesting job.”  
It would say something closer to:

- If the Anthropic role is **mostly** a pay/title increase and the work is materially less interesting, research would lean toward expecting **less day-to-day satisfaction**.
- If the Anthropic role satisfies a deeply important aspiration — “I really want to lead” — then **fit** may outweigh the loss in interest.
- The strongest long-run predictor is usually **not salary alone**, but **whether the role’s daily activities fit what energizes you**.

## If you want concrete papers/lines of research to look at

A short reading list:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — integrative meta-analysis of work design  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
- **Nye et al. (2012)** — vocational interest congruence and outcomes  
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** / later self-determination theory work — autonomy, competence, relatedness at work  
- **Steger / Allan / related meaningful work literature** — meaningful work and well-being  
- **Wrzesniewski et al.** — job, career, and calling orientations

## Bottom line

If you want the most defensible research-based answer in one sentence:

> **On average, people are happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and a good fit for them than in jobs that are merely better paid; leadership increases satisfaction mainly when it fits a genuine personal aspiration, not just because it is a promotion.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision memo based on the literature** — e.g. a 1-page framework with weighted factors: interest, leadership fit, autonomy, meaning, pay, future option value, and likely regret risk.
