answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs. slightly higher salary at Anthropic vs. Google.” The strongest evidence comes from organizational psychology, work design, motivation, and wellbeing research.

## Short version

**On average, people are happier in jobs that are more intrinsically rewarding** — meaning more interesting, more autonomous, more meaningful, and a better fit for their interests/strengths.  
A **moderate pay increase usually matters less** for day-to-day wellbeing and job satisfaction, especially for already high-income workers, than those job-quality factors.

**Direct research on later “regret” is much thinner** than research on satisfaction/wellbeing. But the closest evidence suggests:

- people often value **interesting/meaningful work very highly**,
- **job content and fit** predict satisfaction strongly,
- **pay matters**, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already strong,
- **leadership opportunities** can increase satisfaction if leadership itself is intrinsically meaningful to you, but can reduce it if they mainly add stress and pull you away from the work you enjoy.

---

## The most relevant findings

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting work” is usually broken into things like:

- **skill variety**
- **challenge**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / impact**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn**

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). A large later meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson (2007)** found that these work-design features are strongly associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **intrinsic motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This is one of the most robust findings in work psychology.

### Practical interpretation
If one job is genuinely more intellectually engaging and interesting on a daily basis, research would generally predict higher day-to-day work satisfaction from that job.

---

## 2) Person-job fit matters a lot

A separate line of research looks at **person-environment fit**: how well the job fits your interests, values, identity, and strengths.

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that better fit is associated with:

- more **job satisfaction**
- more **organizational commitment**
- less intent to quit

There is also work on **vocational interest congruence** (Holland/RIASEC tradition): people tend to be more satisfied and more persistent when the work matches what they are actually interested in.

### Practical interpretation
If the Google role fits your interests much better, that is not a “soft” factor — it is one of the main predictors of satisfaction.

---

## 3) Meaningfulness is a major predictor of wellbeing

Research on **meaningful work** consistently finds that it is strongly linked to:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- broader wellbeing
- sometimes even life meaning

There are several reviews/meta-analyses in this area (e.g. work by **Allan** and others; also broader reviews by **Rosso et al.** and **Lysova et al.**).

### Practical interpretation
If “interesting” for you also means “this work feels important and worth doing,” that is especially powerful. Meaning is not just nice-to-have; it is a major predictor of sustained satisfaction.

---

## 4) Salary matters — but usually less than people think, once pay is already high

There are two different literatures here:

### A. Pay and job satisfaction
Research generally finds that **pay level has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction**.  
People care a lot about:

- whether pay feels **fair**
- whether it meets their needs
- how it compares to peers
- whether it signals status/respect

But the raw amount of pay has a smaller effect than many other job characteristics.

### B. Income and wellbeing
The broader happiness literature (e.g. **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**; later work by **Killingsworth**) suggests:

- more income generally helps, especially when it reduces financial stress,
- but the marginal wellbeing gain from additional income tends to diminish,
- and income often affects **life evaluation** more than the quality of your daily work experience.

### Practical interpretation
A **bit higher salary** usually does matter less than:
- what you do all day,
- whether you enjoy it,
- how much autonomy you have,
- whether the work feels meaningful,
- whether the environment is sustainable.

For someone already well-compensated, research would usually predict that a modest salary bump alone is **not** the dominant determinant of happiness.

---

## 5) Autonomy and self-determination are especially important

**Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; applied to work by **Gagné & Deci**, and later reviews by **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan**) says wellbeing at work depends heavily on whether the job supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that support these lead to better motivation and wellbeing than jobs driven mainly by external rewards.

### Why this matters for your case
A team-lead role might increase:
- autonomy
- impact
- relatedness
- sense of growth

But it might also reduce:
- hands-on technical competence-building
- direct contact with the part of the work you currently find interesting

So from a research perspective, “leadership opportunity” is not just an extrinsic perk. It could be deeply intrinsically rewarding **if** it satisfies those psychological needs for you.

---

## 6) Leadership/promotion is mixed, not automatically better

Research on promotions/management roles is less clean than the above areas, but the broad picture is:

- promotions often increase **status, pay, and formal influence**
- leadership roles can increase **autonomy** and **sense of impact**
- but they also often increase **stress, role conflict, administrative work, and time pressure**

So leadership improves satisfaction mainly when:
- the person genuinely wants to manage and develop others,
- the organization supports managers well,
- the role does not completely sever them from the work they find meaningful.

### Practical interpretation
The research does **not** support “team lead is obviously better.”  
It supports: team lead is better **for people who find leading itself meaningful**.

---

## 7) Work quality can matter as much as or more than employment status itself

There is also longitudinal research showing that **poor-quality jobs** — especially high stress, low control, low meaning jobs — can be quite damaging to mental health. Some studies even suggest that certain poor-quality jobs can be **worse for mental health than unemployment**.

The specific papers here are not exactly about your situation, but they reinforce a strong conclusion:

> The quality of the work itself matters a lot, not just whether the job is prestigious or well-paid.

---

## What about regret specifically?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, direct body of research that says:

> “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paying, leadership job later regretted it less/more.”

That exact comparison is not well-studied.

### The closest relevant result
General regret research (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) suggests that **long-term regrets often come more from inaction than action**, especially around missed opportunities for growth or self-definition.

But I would treat that as **secondary evidence**, not as strong as the work-satisfaction literature above.

So if you are asking strictly from the literature:

- **strong evidence:** interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts higher satisfaction
- **strong evidence:** modest pay differences are less important than people often expect
- **moderate evidence:** fit matters a lot
- **mixed evidence:** leadership can be great or bad depending on whether you truly want it
- **weak/direct evidence:** long-run regret on exactly this tradeoff

---

## If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff

Without giving personal advice, the research would say to pay special attention to these questions:

1. **How much more interesting is the Google work, really?**  
   If it’s a large difference, that likely matters a lot.

2. **Is leading a small team itself intrinsically meaningful to you?**  
   If yes, that is not a minor factor.

3. **How much autonomy would you have in each role?**  
   Autonomy is a major predictor of satisfaction.

4. **What will your daily work actually look like?**  
   Daily task content tends to matter more than title.

5. **Is the salary difference large enough to materially change your life?**  
   Research suggests “a bit higher” often does less than expected.

6. **What about workload, pressure, and sustainability?**  
   These strongly affect wellbeing and burnout.

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence:

> **For already well-paid professionals, the nature of the work itself — especially interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit — is usually a stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction than a modest increase in pay.**

The biggest exception is:

> **If the leadership role fulfills a deeply important intrinsic goal for you, then the “less interesting work” may still produce higher satisfaction overall.**

So the literature does **not** reduce your decision to “interesting vs money.”  
It says the real predictors are:

- interestingness / fit
- meaning
- autonomy
- leadership as a source of growth/impact
- workload/stress
- only then, incremental pay

If you want, I can also turn this into a **research-backed decision matrix** with weights for:
- interesting work
- leadership growth
- autonomy
- compensation
- expected stress
- fit/meaning

using only factors that are well-supported in the literature.

answer:
Yes. The short version from the research literature is:

- **Doing work that feels interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to your interests is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Higher pay does improve satisfaction, but usually much less than people expect, especially once compensation is already high.**
- **There is less direct research on “future regret” for choices exactly like yours, but broader regret research suggests people often regret not pursuing personally important possibilities more than they regret giving up extra money.**
- In your case, **“leading a team” is not just an extrinsic perk**; if it is something you genuinely want, research would treat that more like an intrinsic/identity-related factor than like salary.

A more detailed summary:

## 1) “Interesting work” does matter a lot

Researchers usually don’t study “interesting” directly; they study nearby constructs such as:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** like variety, autonomy, task significance, feedback
- **person-job fit** (“this work fits my interests/strengths”)

These are very consistently related to happiness at work.

### Job design research
The classic **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) argues that work is more motivating and satisfying when it provides:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these kinds of work-design features are **substantially associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower turnover intentions**.

So if by “interesting” you mean the work itself is intellectually engaging and feels significant, the evidence says that is not a minor preference — it is a major determinant of how good the job feels.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly tied to well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. (2019)** found that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- stronger **organizational commitment**
- lower **turnover intentions**

The relationship with **job satisfaction** is typically stronger than with life satisfaction, which makes sense: your job affects your whole life, but it affects your work experience most directly.

So if one role feels “very interesting” and the other feels “much less interesting,” that difference is likely to matter in a durable way.

## 3) Person-job fit is a big deal

Another strong result: people are happier when the role fits who they are.

The meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that **person-job fit** is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better performance

That matters here because your decision is not just:
- interesting work vs more money

It is also:
- **deep technical research fit** vs **leadership-role fit**

If leading a small team is something you have “always wanted,” then that is a real dimension of fit. A job can be less technically interesting but still fit better overall if it aligns better with your values, identity, or long-term goals.

## 4) Pay matters, but less than most people think

There is good evidence that **pay level has only a small positive relationship with job satisfaction**.

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay and job satisfaction is positive but fairly small**. In practical terms:

- being underpaid or insecure clearly hurts
- being fairly compensated matters
- but **differences in already-high compensation do not usually explain much of the difference in how happy people are at work**

This is especially relevant in your situation, because both Google and Anthropic likely put you in a salary range where basic financial security is already not the main issue.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means the **marginal happiness return** from “a bit higher salary” is likely to be smaller than the marginal effect of things like:

- daily interest
- autonomy
- growth
- meaning
- manager/team quality
- role fit

## 5) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic incentives for quality of experience

A broad literature around **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; Gagné & Deci; Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan) finds that people do best psychologically when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When these needs are met, people report more:

- motivation
- engagement
- vitality
- well-being

Work that is interesting tends to support **competence** and often **autonomy**. Leadership can support **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness**, but it can also add stress and role conflict. So management is not automatically better or worse — it depends on fit.

## 6) Leadership roles: the evidence is more mixed

On the “leading a small team” aspect, the research is less clean-cut.

Management roles often bring more:

- autonomy
- status
- influence
- skill variety
- sense of impact

But also more:

- interruptions
- people problems
- role conflict
- stress
- less time doing the core work you enjoy

So the average effect of moving into management is **mixed**. The literature suggests it depends heavily on whether the person actually wants the role and is suited to it.

This is important: if leading is a genuine aspiration, then it is **not just compensation/status**. It may be a major source of future satisfaction. But if you mainly enjoy deep technical work and only like the *idea* of leading, the day-to-day reality can disappoint.

## 7) What about regret?

This is where the evidence gets thinner.

I don’t know of strong research that directly answers:
> Do people later regret choosing a more interesting IC role over a slightly better-paid, less interesting leadership role?

But there are two relevant findings:

### A. Long-term regret is often about not pursuing valued possibilities
In classic regret research, **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** found that over the long term, people often regret **inactions** more than actions — especially when those inactions involve not pursuing an important possible self or aspiration.

Applied cautiously: if “leading a team” is a long-standing aspiration, **not trying it** could become a source of regret.

### B. People are bad at forecasting what will make them happy
Affective forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient factors like salary, prestige, or single headline features, and underweight the emotional reality of daily experience.

Applied here: if one job is “very interesting” and the other is “much less interesting,” the day-to-day experiential difference may matter more than the salary number feels like it should.

## 8) One strong conclusion from the literature

If I reduce the literature to one sentence, it would be:

> **Once pay is already high enough, day-to-day work quality — especially intrinsic interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit — tends to matter more for sustained job satisfaction than incremental pay.**

That is probably the clearest evidence-based takeaway.

## 9) But your case has two intrinsic goods, not one

This is the key nuance.

Your choice is not simply:

- **interesting job** vs **higher salary**

It is:

- **more interesting research**
vs
- **less interesting work, but a real chance to lead people**, which you say you have always wanted

Research would likely classify **leading a team** as a potentially meaningful, identity-relevant source of satisfaction, not just an extrinsic perk.

So the evidence does **not** simply say “stay where the work is more interesting.”
It says something more like:

- If the Anthropic role is mainly attractive because of the **salary bump**, research leans toward **staying with the more interesting work**.
- If it is attractive because it fulfills a deep aspiration toward **leadership, autonomy, and broader impact**, then it becomes a much closer call, because those are also strong predictors of satisfaction.

## 10) The best-supported factors to compare, based on research

If you want to make this decision in a research-aligned way, compare the jobs on these dimensions:

1. **Intrinsic interest**  
   How much will you enjoy the actual work 9 months in?

2. **Meaning / significance**  
   Does the work feel worthwhile?

3. **Autonomy**  
   How much control will you have over what and how you work?

4. **Competence growth**  
   Which role will make you better at what you want to become?

5. **Person-role fit**  
   Are you more “research IC” or “leader-builder” at this stage?

6. **Manager and team quality**  
   This is one of the biggest predictors of whether a role feels good.

7. **Pay sufficiency, not just pay level**  
   Will the extra money materially change your life, or is it mainly symbolic?

8. **Option value**  
   If you stay at Google, can you move into leadership later?  
   If you go to Anthropic, can you return to deep research later?

That last one is not a psychology result; it is just a practical decision-theory factor.

## 11) If you want a simple evidence-based summary

Very roughly:

- **Interesting / meaningful / well-fitting work**: strong and consistent link to job satisfaction and well-being
- **More pay (once already highly paid)**: positive but relatively small effect
- **Leadership opportunity**: potentially very important if it matches your long-term identity and aspirations; mixed if not
- **Regret**: limited direct evidence, but people often regret not trying important aspirational paths

## 12) Papers / authors worth looking up

If you want the research trail, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work outcomes  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly robust research literature here, and the broad answer is:

**People tend to be happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomous, and these factors usually predict job satisfaction more strongly than pay differences do** — especially once pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the issue.

A few important details:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, this usually shows up under constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** such as:
  - skill variety
  - task significance
  - autonomy
  - feedback
  - challenge/complexity

### Classic line of research: Job Characteristics Model
Hackman & Oldham’s work, and later meta-analyses, consistently find that jobs with more:

- **autonomy**
- **variety/challenge**
- **significance/meaning**
- **feedback**

produce higher:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics were **moderately to strongly related** to job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower strain.

So if by “interesting” you mean *intellectually engaging, challenging, and meaningful*, the literature is very favorable to that.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but usually less than people think

Pay does matter, but the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is generally **positive but modest**.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay level and job satisfaction** is **fairly weak** — roughly in the “small” range compared with many intrinsic job factors.

In plain English:
- getting paid more tends to help
- but it often helps **less** than:
  - liking the work
  - having autonomy
  - feeling effective
  - having a good manager/team
  - feeling the work matters

This is especially true for people who are already well-compensated. At higher income levels, incremental salary gains often have **much smaller effects** on subjective well-being than day-to-day work experience does.

So the literature does **not** support the idea that a somewhat higher salary will usually compensate for substantially less interesting work.

---

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A more recent meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** on **meaningful work**

found that meaningful work is associated with higher:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction

and lower:

- psychological distress

This matters because “interesting” work and “meaningful” work are not identical, but they often overlap in real careers. If your current work is not just interesting but genuinely meaningful to you, that tends to be a major positive in the literature.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motivation generally beats extrinsic motivation for sustained satisfaction

Another large literature, including **Self-Determination Theory**, finds that people do better when work satisfies basic psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — I have control
- **competence** — I’m growing / good at this
- **relatedness** — I feel connected / part of something

Research in this tradition consistently finds that satisfying these needs predicts:

- better well-being
- more engagement
- lower burnout
- better persistence

That means jobs that are interesting and growth-producing often have a durable advantage over jobs chosen mainly for extrinsic reasons like money or prestige.

---

## 5) “Fit” matters a lot: the right answer depends on what kind of interest you mean

This is important for your case.

You described two different possible sources of satisfaction:

1. **interesting technical/research content**
2. **leading a small team**, which you say you’ve always wanted

Research on **person-job fit** and **interest congruence** shows that people are happier when the job matches their stable interests and values.

Meta-analytic work on vocational fit (for example, the Holland-type literature; see work by **Nye and colleagues**) generally finds that better fit predicts higher:

- job satisfaction
- persistence
- sometimes performance

So from a research perspective, your choice is not just:

- interesting work vs more money

It may be:

- **technical intrinsic interest** vs **leadership intrinsic interest**, plus a salary bump

That’s a more complex tradeoff.

If leading people is a deep aspiration rather than just a status marker, then the Anthropic role may also satisfy important **intrinsic** motives, not just extrinsic ones.

---

## 6) What about regret specifically?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of strong, direct longitudinal research that asks exactly:

> “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid less interesting one?”

That specific comparison has not been studied nearly as cleanly as job satisfaction and well-being.

### What related regret research suggests
The broader regret literature — especially work by **Gilovich & Medvec** and later researchers — often finds:

- in the **short term**, people regret actions more
- in the **long term**, people more often regret **inactions** and paths not taken

But that is **general regret research**, not career-specific proof that you would regret staying or switching.

A cautious interpretation would be:

- There is **good evidence** that day-to-day happiness is higher in work that is intrinsically engaging and meaningful.
- There is **much less direct evidence** on whether people later regret prioritizing interesting work.
- General regret research suggests people often regret **not pursuing valued possibilities**, but that shouldn’t be overextended.

So if you want a strictly evidence-based summary:
- **happiness/satisfaction evidence**: strong
- **regret evidence for this exact tradeoff**: limited

---

## 7) What the literature would predict in a situation like yours

Very cautiously, and sticking to research rather than advice:

### If you stay in the more interesting research role
Research would predict higher outcomes if that role gives you more:
- intrinsic interest
- mastery
- meaning
- engagement
- identity fit

That usually translates into:
- higher job satisfaction
- lower risk of disengagement
- possibly better long-term well-being

### If you take the Anthropic role
Research would predict better outcomes if the new role gives you more:
- autonomy
- leadership/identity fulfillment
- influence
- growth into a desired future self
- sense of significance

That could also raise satisfaction — **even if the actual work content is less interesting** — if leadership is a strong personal value.

### What the literature would be skeptical about
It would be skeptical of a move that is mainly:
- somewhat more pay
- somewhat more prestige
- but clearly less interesting and less aligned with your intrinsic motivations

Those moves often do **less** for happiness than people expect.

---

## 8) Probably the most evidence-based summary sentence

If I had to compress the literature into one line:

> **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work tends to contribute more to sustained job happiness than modest differences in salary do; however, role fit matters, and if leadership is itself a deeply valued intrinsic goal for you, that can be just as important as the interestingness of the technical work.**

---

## 9) A few papers worth looking at

If you want the actual research trail, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.
- **Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014).** Intrinsic motivation and performance: A meta-analysis.
- Work on **Self-Determination Theory** in organizations, including reviews/meta-analyses by **Van den Broeck** and colleagues.
- Meta-analytic work on **person-vocation fit / interest congruence**, e.g. **Nye** and colleagues.

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **short annotated reading list** with the main findings of each paper, or  
2. translate this literature into a **decision framework** that stays evidence-based and avoids “gut feel” advice.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs slightly higher salary.” In the literature, “interesting work” is usually captured by constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job characteristics**: autonomy, variety, challenge, feedback
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit**

The short version of the evidence is:

1. **Interesting / engaging / meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being.**
2. **Pay matters, but its association with job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect**, especially once someone is already well-paid.
3. **Leadership opportunity can matter a lot too** — but mostly when it matches the person’s values and identity, i.e. if they genuinely want to lead rather than just “advance.”
4. **Direct evidence on “regret” for this exact tradeoff is limited.** Most studies look at satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover, not retrospective regret.

## 1) Interesting work predicts satisfaction quite strongly

A foundational result in organizational psychology is that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

This is the **Job Characteristics Model** from **Hackman & Oldham (1970s)**, and later meta-analyses broadly supported the idea.

### Key result
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these “motivational job characteristics” are **moderately to strongly related** to:
- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

In plainer English: **people tend to be happier in jobs that are more interesting, more autonomous, and feel more meaningful.**

Among these factors, **autonomy** is especially strong and consistent.

## 2) Pay matters, but less than intrinsic job quality

There is a common intuition that salary should dominate. The empirical literature usually does **not** support that, at least for job satisfaction.

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. The correlation is real, but relatively small compared with what many people assume.

### The practical reading
If someone is moving from financial stress to financial security, pay has a big effect.
If someone is already in a highly compensated role — which sounds likely in your case — then a **“bit higher salary”** usually predicts **much less additional satisfaction** than differences in:
- interest
- autonomy
- meaning
- growth
- fit

So, for people already at high income, **job content tends to matter more than a modest pay bump**.

## 3) Meaningful work has a large association with well-being

There’s also a big literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** and related researchers shows that perceiving work as meaningful is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- better overall well-being

This is one of the more robust findings in work psychology.

If your current Google role feels “very interesting” in a deep sense — intellectually alive, engaging, meaningful — the literature would generally predict that this is a major contributor to satisfaction.

## 4) Intrinsic motives tend to outperform extrinsic motives for well-being

Another relevant stream is **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent work on **intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation**.

The broad result is that people tend to do better psychologically when work satisfies needs for:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

This usually means that jobs are better for well-being when they feel:
- self-directed
- challenging in a growth-producing way
- connected to other people or a valued purpose

Work pursued mainly for **extrinsic rewards** — money, prestige, status signals — is not necessarily bad, but on average it predicts **less durable well-being** than work aligned with intrinsic motives.

That said, your case is not “interesting work vs pure money.”  
You also mention **leading a small team**, which is itself often an **intrinsic aspiration**:
- more responsibility
- more impact
- more development
- more identity fit

So from a research perspective, your tradeoff is probably more like:

**intellectual interest / deep content fit**  
vs  
**leadership aspiration / growth / impact**

That’s an important distinction.

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot

A strong meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are reliably associated with:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better overall attitudes toward work

This means there probably isn’t a universal answer like “always pick the more interesting work.”  
The more research-based answer is:

> People do better when the job fits what they actually value.

So if you deeply value:
- doing frontier research
- intellectual novelty
- technically fascinating problems

then staying may fit better.

If you deeply value:
- leadership
- building teams
- shaping direction through others
- transitioning from individual contributor to manager/lead

then the Anthropic role may fit better, even if the subject matter is less interesting.

## 6) What about regret?

This is where the evidence is thinner.

I’m not aware of strong, direct studies answering:
> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the better-paid leadership role?”

That exact comparison is not a standard research question.

### What the literature does say indirectly
- People in jobs with **low intrinsic quality** tend to have lower satisfaction and higher turnover intentions.
- People whose jobs fit their values and identity tend to do better over time.
- In broader regret research, people often regret **paths not taken**, especially around growth and self-development — but that literature is broad and not specific enough to confidently tell you which choice you’d regret.

So if you want a strict evidence-based answer:  
**There is strong evidence about happiness/satisfaction, weak evidence about regret specifically.**

## A research-based summary for your exact case

If I compress the literature down to something decision-relevant:

### What the evidence most strongly supports
- **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work strongly predicts satisfaction.**
- **Small-to-moderate salary differences predict much less satisfaction.**
- **Fit matters more than prestige or generic “advancement.”**
- **Leadership opportunities are valuable when leadership itself is a genuine intrinsic goal.**

### Therefore
If the Anthropic offer is:
- only **a bit** more money,
- but **substantially less interesting** day-to-day,

then the literature would generally predict that **the more interesting role is likely to produce higher day-to-day job satisfaction**, especially for someone already financially comfortable.

### But
If leading a team is something you have **wanted for a long time**, that matters a lot, because leadership itself may be one of your strongest intrinsic motivators. In that case, the new role is not merely “less interesting”; it may be **more aligned with a different important value**.

## The closest thing to a research-based decision rule

If you want a very evidence-aligned way to think about it, compare the two roles on these dimensions:

1. **Autonomy**
2. **Learning / challenge**
3. **Meaning / significance**
4. **Person-job fit**
5. **Growth toward your desired future self**
6. **Relationship quality / people**
7. **Compensation**
8. **Risk / stress / workload**

Research suggests that **1–5 usually drive satisfaction more strongly than 7**, once pay is already high enough.

## Papers / authors worth looking up

If you want to read the underlying literature, these are good places to start:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work
- **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory**; also work on need satisfaction at work

If you want, I can also do one of two things:

1. give you a **more formal literature summary with citations and key findings**, or  
2. help translate this research into a **decision framework tailored to your specific situation** without giving personal-opinion advice.

answer:
Yes. There is quite a lot of research on this, though usually not under the exact phrase “interesting work.” In the literature it’s usually studied as:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job design / work characteristics**
- **autonomy, variety, skill use, learning**
- **person-job fit**

### Short version

The best-supported finding is:

> **The day-to-day nature of the work itself — especially whether it is interesting, autonomous, meaningful, and uses your skills — is usually a stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction and well-being than salary differences, once pay is already good.**

That does **not** mean money or promotion opportunities do not matter. It means that for long-run happiness at work, the intrinsic qualities of the job tend to matter more.

---

## What the research says

### 1. “Interesting” work is strongly tied to job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later tested in meta-analyses.

The core idea: jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have more:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are very close to what most people mean by “interesting work.”

#### Key result
Meta-analyses found that these job characteristics have **moderate to strong relationships** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

Their conclusion was basically that work design matters a lot, and “motivational” job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of positive work attitudes.

A previous meta-analysis:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)**

found similar support.

### 2. Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of positive work outcomes

Another large literature studies **meaningful work**.

#### Key result
People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report more:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- overall well-being
- commitment

and less:

- burnout
- turnover intention

A useful summary is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work

The broad pattern is strong and very consistent: meaning at work is not a “nice extra”; it is one of the central correlates of good work-related well-being.

---

### 3. Pay matters — but usually less than people expect

There is also meta-analytic research on pay and job satisfaction.

#### Key result
**Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest.**

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**

found that the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **real but relatively small** compared with many intrinsic job features.

The rough takeaway from this literature is:

- pay matters
- **pay fairness** matters a lot
- relative pay/comparison matters
- but **salary alone is not a strong predictor of whether people actually like their jobs**

So if the choice is “somewhat higher pay” versus “substantially more interesting work,” the research generally leans toward the latter as the better predictor of long-run job satisfaction.

---

### 4. Intrinsic motives tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic motives

There is a broader literature in motivation psychology — especially **Self-Determination Theory** — showing that people do better when their work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

This framework consistently finds that intrinsically motivated activity is linked to higher well-being than activity pursued mainly for extrinsic rewards.

Relevant lines of work:

- **Deci & Ryan** / Self-Determination Theory
- reviews/meta-analyses on need satisfaction at work

#### Key result
When work feels self-endorsed, growth-promoting, and competence-building, people tend to be:

- happier
- more engaged
- less burned out

This matters for your case because “interesting research” is not just a preference; in the literature it often counts as an **intrinsic motivator**, which tends to have durable effects.

---

### 5. Person-job fit is very important

This is especially relevant for your situation because “leading a small team” may itself be intrinsically meaningful for you.

The literature on **person-job fit** and **person-environment fit** finds that satisfaction depends heavily on whether the role matches what the person values and enjoys.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

found that fit is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit

#### Important implication
There is no universal answer like “interesting IC work is always better than management.”

The evidence suggests something more specific:

> **The better predictor of future satisfaction is the option whose daily activities fit your intrinsic motives better.**

So if what you really value is:
- solving hard research problems yourself, the research points toward staying closer to the interesting work;
- developing people, coordinating work, and leading, then the leadership role may count as intrinsically rewarding despite being less technically interesting.

That distinction matters a lot.

---

## What about regret?

This is the part where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research asking exactly:

> “Do people later regret choosing higher pay / promotion over more interesting work?”

There is much less direct evidence on **regret** than on satisfaction, burnout, and turnover.

### Best indirect evidence
The best indirect evidence comes from:

1. **Intrinsic vs extrinsic values research**
2. **Longitudinal work on job satisfaction**
3. **Adaptation research**

Those literatures generally suggest:

- gains from pay/title often **adapt**
- the quality of everyday work experience keeps mattering because it repeats every day
- people who prioritize intrinsic qualities of work often report better long-term well-being than those focused mainly on extrinsic rewards

So while I would not claim “people who choose salary regret it,” the research does suggest:

> **People often underestimate how much daily task enjoyment matters and overestimate how much salary/title will compensate for less satisfying work content.**

---

## What about moving into management?

This is more mixed.

Research on promotions/leadership suggests they can bring:

- more status
- more influence
- sometimes more autonomy
- developmental gains

But also:

- more stress
- more coordination/admin work
- less time on the core task
- higher emotional demands

The average effect is not uniformly positive.

A big moderator is whether the person has strong **motivation to lead**. There is research on this construct (e.g. **Chan & Drasgow, 2001**).

#### Translation into evidence terms
If you have “always wanted to lead a small team,” that is not a trivial side factor. It may be part of your intrinsic motivation profile, not merely a prestige motive.

So in research language, your tradeoff is probably not:

- interesting work vs money

It may be closer to:

- **interesting technical work**
vs
- **leadership/mentorship/autonomy/status/growth**

That is a more complex tradeoff, and the literature would say **fit** matters more than any universal rule.

---

## If I had to summarize the empirical picture in one table

| Factor | Typical relationship to job satisfaction / well-being | What the literature generally says |
|---|---:|---|
| Interesting / meaningful / skill-using work | Strong | One of the strongest predictors |
| Autonomy | Strong | Very important for motivation and satisfaction |
| Person-job fit | Strong | Often as important as any single job feature |
| Salary level | Small to modest | Matters, but less than many people assume |
| Pay fairness | Moderate | Often more important than absolute pay |
| Promotion / leadership title | Mixed | Can help, but often comes with higher stress |
| Intrinsic motivation | Strong | Usually better for sustained well-being than extrinsic motives |

---

## Bottom-line research answer

If your current pay is already high and both options are financially comfortable, then the research generally suggests:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work predicts sustained satisfaction better than incremental pay increases.**
2. **Leadership can improve satisfaction if leading is itself intrinsically meaningful to you.**
3. **Direct “regret” evidence is limited, but indirect evidence suggests people often undervalue everyday task enjoyment and overvalue salary/title.**
4. **The strongest general predictor is not money or prestige alone, but fit between the role’s daily activities and your intrinsic motives.**

---

## Useful papers / names to look up

If you want to read the literature directly, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of job characteristics
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work
- **Deci & Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory / intrinsic motivation
- **Chan & Drasgow (2001)** — motivation to lead

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **clean bibliography with short summaries** of the strongest papers, or  
2. translate this literature into a **decision framework** using only research-backed factors.

answer:
Yes. There’s a fairly robust research literature on this, mostly from organizational psychology, motivation research, and well-being research.

## Short version

The broad result is:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work** is usually **one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained well-being**.
- **Pay matters**, but the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is usually **much smaller** than the relationship for intrinsic job qualities.
- **Leadership / promotion** tends to help **somewhat on average**, but its effect is **more mixed** because it also brings stress, admin work, and less time on the “interesting” part of the job.
- The biggest moderator is **fit**: if leading a team is something you deeply want, that can matter a lot.

So if your question is “does research suggest people are happier staying in the more interesting job?”, the answer is roughly:

> **Often yes, especially when the pay difference is not life-changing and the person is already well-compensated.**  
> But if the alternative role better matches a strong personal value/identity goal—like wanting to lead people—then **fit can outweigh pure interestingness**.

---

## What the research says

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics strongly predict satisfaction

A classic result in work psychology is that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- often also **learning/challenge**

This is the old **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses have generally supported it.

A very influential meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  looked across a very large body of work-design studies and found that motivational job characteristics like **autonomy, task significance, skill variety, and feedback** had **moderate to strong relationships** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - intrinsic motivation
  - engagement
  - lower turnover intentions

In practical terms: **how the work feels day-to-day** matters a lot.

This is probably the closest research match to your “interesting work” question.

---

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There’s also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work in this area finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- lower **turnover intention**
- less **burnout/distress**

A useful reference here is:

- **Allan et al.** (meta-analytic work on meaningful work outcomes)

The exact numbers vary by study, but the general magnitude is usually **moderate**, and often stronger than the effect of pay level.

So if by “interesting” you mean some mix of:
- intellectually stimulating
- aligned with your values
- worth caring about
- something you’d naturally think about even if you didn’t have to

…then the literature generally treats that as a **serious contributor to well-being**, not a luxury.

---

### 3. Pay level matters, but less than most people expect

There is research directly on **pay and job satisfaction**.

The big takeaway is:

- **Pay level** is positively related to job satisfaction,
- but the relationship is usually **small** relative to intrinsic job characteristics.

A commonly cited summary is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**

The rough finding is that the correlation between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with the job** is typically only **small**.  
By contrast, things like autonomy, fit, meaning, and work design are often **substantially stronger** predictors.

Important nuance:
- **Pay satisfaction** and **perceived fairness** matter more than raw pay level.
- Extra money matters a lot when it changes security, debt, housing, family stress, etc.
- But once someone is already well-paid, the **marginal happiness gain** from “a bit more” tends to be smaller.

That last point is also consistent with broader income/well-being research:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)**

Those papers are about income and well-being generally, not jobs specifically, but they support the idea that more money helps, yet **daily experienced well-being depends a lot on daily experience**, not just compensation.

For someone already in a highly paid role at Google, this is especially relevant:  
a “bit higher salary” may matter much less than your brain predicts unless it changes something materially important.

---

### 4. Intrinsic goals tend to beat extrinsic goals for well-being

There is also a broader motivational literature showing that people who prioritize **intrinsic goals**—like growth, learning, relationships, contribution, mastery—tend to do better on well-being than people who primarily chase **extrinsic goals** like money, status, or image.

Key names here:

- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
- **Self-Determination Theory** more broadly  
  (e.g. **Deci & Ryan**; review at work by **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan, 2017**)

At work, self-determination theory says people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often feeds autonomy + competence.  
Leading a team may feed relatedness + significance + autonomy, depending on the role.

This is one reason your choice is genuinely hard: both options may satisfy different psychological needs.

---

### 5. Fit matters a lot

One of the strongest literatures in organizational psychology is **person-job fit** / **person-organization fit**.

People are happier and stay longer when the role matches:
- their interests
- values
- strengths
- identity

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

Their overall conclusion: **fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit**.

This matters a lot in your case because “leading a small team” is not just an external reward. It might be part of your self-concept.

So if team leadership is a real aspiration—not just prestige, but something you’ve wanted because you enjoy mentoring, setting direction, multiplying impact—then the fit literature says that is a **real source of satisfaction**, not a superficial one.

---

### 6. Promotion/leadership: positive on average, but mixed

The research on promotions and moving into leadership is less clean than the work-design literature, but the pattern is roughly:

- Promotions often increase satisfaction **somewhat**
- but also increase:
  - stress
  - hours
  - administrative burden
  - role conflict
  - distance from the technical work itself

There are panel studies suggesting promotions can raise job satisfaction, but part of the effect seems tied to:
- recognition
- future earnings expectations
- status

And the effect is not always huge or permanent.

A useful paper here is:

- **Kosteas (2011)** on promotions and job satisfaction

So the evidence does **not** say “promotion/management makes people happier, full stop.”  
It says more like:

> Leadership can increase satisfaction if it matches what the person wants, but it often trades away enjoyable task content.

That seems almost exactly your situation.

---

## What about regret?

This is where the literature is thinner.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research specifically showing:

- “people who stay in the more interesting job regret it less”
versus
- “people who take higher pay / leadership regret it less”

That exact comparison is not very well studied.

What *is* relevant:

### A) People often mispredict what will make them happier
Research on **affective forecasting** suggests people often overestimate how much lasting happiness they’ll get from major changes in salary, status, or title.

Key names:
- **Gilbert**
- **Wilson**

This happens partly because of:
- **focalism**: over-focusing on the salient difference now
- **hedonic adaptation**: getting used to the new salary/title faster than expected

So there is good reason to be cautious about thoughts like:
- “the raise will feel amazing”
- “finally leading a team will settle something deep”
- “the less interesting work won’t matter much”

People are often wrong in exactly those directions.

### B) Daily experience tends to matter a lot
If a job is less interesting every day, that cost is paid repeatedly.

This is not exactly “regret research,” but it’s consistent with well-being work:
- repeated daily experience has large cumulative effects
- people systematically underweight those effects when making decisions

So the research leans toward:
> Don’t underestimate the value of enjoying the actual work you do most days.

---

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

**The best-supported finding is that intrinsic features of work—interestingness, meaning, autonomy, challenge, and fit—usually matter more for long-term job satisfaction than modest differences in pay, while leadership opportunities help mainly when they strongly fit the person’s values and identity.**

---

## A research-based way to frame your specific choice

Without giving personal advice, the literature suggests these are the highest-value distinctions:

### The Google option is favored by research if:
- the work is **substantially more interesting**
- you’d keep strong autonomy/learning
- the Anthropic salary bump is **not life-changing**
- leading people is a “nice to have” rather than a core personal goal

### The Anthropic option is favored by research if:
- leading a team is a **deeply important identity fit**
- you’d gain autonomy, significance, mentoring, and influence
- the work is less interesting but still sufficiently engaging
- the move advances a longer-term career path you genuinely value

In other words, the research does **not** reduce this to:
- interesting work vs money

It’s more like:
- **interesting work + meaning + autonomy**
vs
- **leadership fit + increased impact + some money**

And the literature says both sides are real, but **money itself is usually the weakest of the three**.

---

## Useful papers / starting points

If you want papers to look up, I’d start here:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

2. **Fried & Ferris (1987)**  
   *The validity of the job characteristics model: A review and meta-analysis.*

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

4. **Judge et al. (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

5. **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**  
   *Self-determination theory in work organizations: The state of a science.*

6. **Kasser & Ryan**  
   on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

7. **Kosteas (2011)**  
   on promotions and job satisfaction

8. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)**  
   on income and well-being

9. **Allan et al.**  
   meta-analytic work on meaningful work and its outcomes

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more academic summary with actual effect sizes**, or  
2. turn this literature into a **decision checklist grounded in research** rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, mostly from organizational psychology, motivation, and well-being research.

The short version is:

- **Interesting/meaningful work is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of job satisfaction and well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high enough and feels fair.**
- **“Fit” matters a lot**: if leading people is something you deeply want, a management role may improve fit even if the subject matter is less interesting.
- **Direct evidence on “regret” is much thinner** than evidence on satisfaction, burnout, engagement, and turnover.

## 1) Interesting work is strongly linked to satisfaction

A classic result is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham): jobs are more satisfying when they offer things like

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- a sense of meaningfulness

A large later meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these kinds of work-design features are strongly related to outcomes like job satisfaction, engagement, internal motivation, and lower strain.

So the literature does support a pretty robust claim:

> People tend to be happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomous.

That does **not** mean “interesting topic” is the only thing that matters. A job can be intellectually interesting but still miserable if it has poor management, low autonomy, or chronic overload.

## 2) Pay has a smaller relationship with job satisfaction than most people assume

One very relevant finding: a meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.

That is important for your case. If you’re already in a high-paying role at Google, then a “bit higher salary” at Anthropic is likely to matter **much less** for happiness than differences in:

- daily work content
- autonomy
- manager/team quality
- meaning/purpose
- stress/load
- fit with your desired career identity

This fits a broader well-being result: money matters a lot when it changes **security** or relieves major stress, but the incremental impact tends to diminish at higher income levels.

So the research would generally say:

> If both options are already financially comfortable, work quality tends to matter more than a modest pay bump.

## 3) Meaningful work predicts well-being quite strongly

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic work here generally finds that perceiving work as meaningful is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- greater engagement
- lower burnout/distress

So if by “interesting” you mean something like:
- “I care about the problems”
- “I’m energized by the work”
- “I’d voluntarily think about this stuff”
- “it feels intellectually alive”

that tends to be a meaningful predictor, not a soft or superficial preference.

## 4) But “wanting to lead” may be equally important — because fit matters

Another strong literature is **person-job / person-organization fit**. A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** found that better fit predicts:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better well-being overall

This matters because your tradeoff is not simply:

- interesting work vs money

It is also:

- **individual contributor work** vs **leading a small team**

If leading people is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, then the Anthropic role may improve **fit**, even if the domain is less interesting.

Research would frame this as:
- A person who values coaching, decision-making, coordination, and growing others may be happier in leadership.
- A person who mostly likes the **idea** of leadership, but actually loves doing deep technical work, may be less happy after switching.

That distinction matters a lot.

## 5) Self-determination research: autonomy, competence, relatedness

**Self-Determination Theory** is another very relevant framework. It argues that well-being at work depends heavily on whether work supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have real choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and challenged
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Across many studies, satisfaction of these needs predicts better motivation and well-being.

Applied to your case:

- **Google current role** may be higher on **competence** and intrinsic interest.
- **Anthropic lead role** may be higher on **autonomy**, influence, and perhaps relatedness.
- The key question is which needs are currently more underfed for you.

If you are already well paid and highly competent, but feel blocked from leadership and broader impact, then the team-lead opportunity could matter more than it superficially seems.

## 6) Promotions/leadership do not reliably make people happier

There isn’t a clean universal result that “becoming a manager makes people happier.”

The evidence is more mixed:

- Promotions often improve satisfaction **somewhat**
- but added stress, less control over your own time, more meetings/conflict, and less hands-on work can offset that
- the effect depends strongly on whether the new role matches what the person actually wants

So the research does **not** support a simplistic view that:
> higher title + more pay = more happiness

Often it’s more like:
> higher title helps only if the day-to-day work aligns with your motives.

That is especially relevant for technical people.

## 7) On regret specifically: evidence is thinner

If your question is literally:

> Do people who stay in the more interesting job later regret not taking the more lucrative/leadership job?

there is much less direct research.

Most job research measures:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover
- mental health
- organizational commitment

not retrospective regret years later.

There is some general psychology research suggesting that, over the long run, people often regret **opportunities not taken** more than actions taken — but that finding is broad and not specific enough to answer your case well.

So I would be careful here:
- **There is strong evidence about happiness/satisfaction.**
- **There is weaker evidence about later regret.**

## Best evidence-based summary

If I had to compress the literature into one decision-relevant statement, it would be:

> For people who are already well compensated, long-term job well-being tends to depend more on intrinsic interest, meaningfulness, autonomy, fit, and relationship quality than on modest differences in salary.

But there is an important exception:

> If leading a team is itself a deeply valued goal, then the leadership opportunity may improve person-job fit enough to outweigh lower subject-matter interest.

So the research does **not** say “always stay with the more interesting work.”
It says more like:

1. **Modest pay differences usually matter less than intrinsic job quality.**
2. **Leadership helps if you truly want the work of leadership, not just the status of it.**
3. **Fit is the central variable.**

## Papers/results worth looking at

If you want the most relevant literature to read, I’d start with:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on **work design**
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**
- **Hackman & Oldham** — classic work on **job characteristics**
- Reviews/meta-analyses on **meaningful work** and **self-determination theory at work**

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you:
1. a **more academic summary with actual citations**, or  
2. a **decision matrix based directly on these research factors**.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad result is fairly consistent:

**Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being.**  
**Pay matters too, but its relationship with job satisfaction is usually much smaller once compensation is already “good enough” and perceived as fair.**

A few research streams are especially relevant.

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied through things like:

- **task variety**
- **skill use**
- **autonomy/control**
- **task significance / meaningfulness**
- **feedback / learning**
- **intrinsic motivation**

### Key finding
Meta-analytic work on **job design** finds that these features are among the strongest correlates of:

- **job satisfaction**
- **intrinsic motivation**
- **engagement**
- **lower burnout / turnover intention**

A classic source is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design research.

Their general conclusion: jobs high in **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback** are much more satisfying and motivating than jobs low on those dimensions.

This has been replicated many times in related frameworks.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is a strong predictor of well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

### Key finding
People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- better **mental well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**

A useful summary is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work.

The general size of these associations is not tiny; they are usually in the **moderate-to-strong** range by psychology standards.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I actually care about the content and find it engaging,” the literature says that is not a trivial preference — it is a major contributor to work well-being.

---

## 3) Autonomy matters a lot

Another very robust result: **autonomy/control** is one of the strongest job features for satisfaction.

This comes up in:

- **Job Characteristics Model** literature
- **Self-Determination Theory**
- **Job Demands–Resources** research

### Key finding
When work supports:
- **autonomy** (“I have control / discretion”)
- **competence** (“I’m growing / effective”)
- **relatedness** (“I feel connected / respected”)

people tend to have better well-being and motivation.

Relevant synthesis:

- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** — meta-analytic review of basic psychological needs at work.

This matters for your case because **leading a small team** may increase autonomy/competence/relatedness for you — but only if you actually want that role and the org supports it well.

---

## 4) Pay matters, but less than people often think for job satisfaction

There is research specifically on **pay and job satisfaction**.

### Key finding
The relationship is **positive but modest**.

A widely cited source:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

The broad conclusion is that **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction, but only weakly to moderately**.  
In other words:

- money is **not irrelevant**
- but it is usually **far from the dominant determinant** of whether someone likes their job

This is especially important in your situation, because the tradeoff you describe is **not low pay vs high pay**. It is **high pay vs somewhat higher pay**.

At already-high compensation levels, the **marginal utility of extra salary** often appears smaller than the effect of daily job experience.

---

## 5) Income helps overall well-being, but the story is nuanced

There is also broader happiness research on income.

Older work:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** suggested emotional well-being plateaus around a certain income level.

Newer work:

- **Killingsworth (2021)** and later joint work suggest well-being can continue rising with income on average, though not always linearly and with a lot of heterogeneity.

### What is safe to conclude?
- More income generally helps.
- But **income is only one input** into well-being.
- In high-income populations, **job quality** often matters enormously.
- Also, people adapt somewhat to income changes.

So the research does **not** support “salary doesn’t matter.”  
But it also does **not** support “take the higher-paying job and you’ll probably be happier.”

---

## 6) Person-job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction

A strong literature on **person-environment fit** asks whether the role matches:

- your interests
- your values
- your skills
- your identity
- your preferred work style

### Key finding
Better fit predicts:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intent to quit

Classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

This is especially relevant to your case because there are **two different kinds of fit** in tension:

1. **content fit**: the work itself is more interesting at Google  
2. **role fit**: leading a small team may fit a long-standing aspiration better at Anthropic

Research would predict that whichever option better matches your deeper motives is more likely to feel satisfying over time.

---

## 7) What about regret?

This is the part where the literature is weaker.

There is **much less direct research** asking:

> “Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid one?”

I don’t know of a strong, canonical body of work that answers that exact question cleanly.

What the evidence does show indirectly is:

- people regret decisions less when choices align with **intrinsic values** and **identity**
- **value congruence** and **person-job fit** predict satisfaction and lower turnover
- **extrinsic gains** like money or status often produce less durable satisfaction than expected
- people often adapt to salary/status gains, while daily task experience keeps affecting them every day

So:  
**direct regret evidence is limited**, but the surrounding evidence suggests that choosing against your intrinsic interests can be costly for longer-term satisfaction.

---

## 8) One important complication: leadership itself may be intrinsically rewarding

Your case is not just “interesting work vs salary.”

It sounds more like:

- **Google**: more interesting technical/research content
- **Anthropic**: somewhat higher pay + chance to lead a team, which you’ve wanted

From a research perspective, **leading a team** is not merely an extrinsic perk. It can satisfy:

- autonomy
- competence
- status
- growth
- identity aspirations

So if leading is something you genuinely want, it may itself be a source of intrinsic motivation.

That means the real tradeoff may be:

- **interesting content**
vs
- **interesting role / developmental trajectory**

Research doesn’t say content always wins. It says **intrinsic factors** usually matter a lot, and often more than incremental pay.

Leadership could count as one of those intrinsic factors if it is central to your self-concept.

---

## 9) If you want the strongest evidence-based summary

If I compress the literature into a few defensible statements:

### Very well supported
1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
2. **Person-job fit strongly predicts satisfaction and retention.**
3. **Pay predicts satisfaction, but much less strongly than many people assume.**
4. **At high compensation levels, incremental salary increases often buy less happiness than better day-to-day work experience.**

### Also likely true
5. **People adapt somewhat to raises and status gains.**
6. **Non-pay job attributes often drive choice and well-being more than people initially expect.**

### Less firmly answered
7. **Exact long-run regret rates for choosing “interesting work” over “better-paid leadership role” are not well pinned down by research.**

---

## 10) The research-based way to think about your decision

If you want to stay close to the evidence, compare the two jobs on these variables:

- **Intrinsic interest in daily work**
- **Autonomy / control**
- **Meaning / significance**
- **Learning and growth**
- **Fit with your long-term identity**
- **Manager/team quality**
- **Workload / stress / uncertainty**
- **Pay and perceived fairness**
- **Whether leadership is intrinsically important to you or just symbolically attractive**

That list is closer to the research than simply asking “salary vs interestingness.”

---

## 11) Practical bottom line from the literature

If your current Google role is substantially more interesting, and both roles already pay very well, the literature would generally predict that **staying in the more interesting role is often better for day-to-day job satisfaction**.

But if **leading a team is itself a deeply important intrinsic goal** for you, then the Anthropic role may not be “less interesting” in the way the research usually means. It may be less interesting in **content**, but more satisfying in **autonomy, growth, and identity fit**.

So the best research-based framing is:

> **Don’t compare salary to interest. Compare the full intrinsic bundle of each role.**

---

## A few references to look up

- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)**. Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)**. The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)**. Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019)**. Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.  
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** on basic psychological need satisfaction at work.  
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** on income and well-being.

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more academic summary** with effect sizes and stronger caveats, or  
2. turn this into a **decision sheet grounded in the literature** for your exact choice.
