answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

**The “work itself” — interestingness, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit with your identity — is usually a stronger and more durable predictor of job satisfaction than salary level, especially once salary is already high enough to feel fair and secure.**  
There is **much less direct research on regret** from this exact kind of choice, but the evidence we do have points in the same direction: **misfit with your interests/values tends to produce more persistent dissatisfaction than giving up some extra pay.**

A few of the main findings:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

### Classic result: the nature of the work matters a lot
A long line of research — starting with **Herzberg** and then more rigorously with **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** — finds that people are more satisfied and motivated when work has:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- a sense of doing a whole, meaningful piece of work

These are strongly related to:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout/withdrawal

A major meta-analysis in work design research (**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**) found that **autonomy and meaningful job characteristics have substantial associations with satisfaction and motivation**.

### Translation to your case
If your current Google role is “very interesting” in the sense that it gives you challenge, intellectual engagement, and a sense of progress, research says that is not a trivial perk; it is one of the central ingredients of sustainable work satisfaction.

---

## 2) Intrinsic motivation predicts well-being better than extrinsic rewards

### Self-Determination Theory
A huge literature around **Deci & Ryan’s Self-Determination Theory** finds that people do better when work satisfies three psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — I have choice/control
- **competence** — I’m getting better / using my abilities well
- **relatedness** — I feel connected and effective with others

Across many studies, need satisfaction at work is linked to:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- better well-being
- less burnout

This matters because **interesting work usually feeds autonomy and competence**.  
But note: **leading a team** can also feed autonomy, competence, and relatedness — if it is genuinely something you want, not just a status move.

---

## 3) Salary matters, but less than most people think for job satisfaction

One of the clearest meta-analytic results here is:

### Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction
A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the correlation between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**. Roughly speaking, salary explains only a **small fraction** of why some people are satisfied and others are not.

What matters more than raw pay:
- whether pay feels **fair**
- whether it removes financial stress
- whether it signals recognition/status in a way you care about

At already-high compensation levels, the incremental well-being gain from “a bit higher salary” is usually much smaller than the gain/loss from:
- enjoying the work
- autonomy
- learning
- mission/meaning
- manager/team quality

So if the Anthropic offer is only “a bit higher,” the literature would generally say: **don’t overweight that part**.

---

## 4) Meaningful work has a strong relationship with well-being

There are also meta-analyses on **meaningful work** showing that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction / psychological well-being
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intention

This overlaps with “interesting work,” but not perfectly. Some work is interesting but not meaningful; some is meaningful but not intellectually exciting.

In research roles, people often care about both:
- **Is the problem fascinating?**
- **Does it matter?**

If the new role is “much less interesting,” that is a meaningful negative in the literature, especially if your identity is tied to research depth.

---

## 5) Person-job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction

A large literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** shows:

- when the job fits your interests, values, and preferred way of working, satisfaction is higher
- misfit predicts dissatisfaction and turnover

This is important because your choice is not just between:
- interesting work
- higher pay
- leadership

It is also between **two different identities**:
- researcher / deep technical contributor
- leader / manager-builder

If “leading a small team” is something you’ve “always wanted,” that may reflect a real and important **fit variable**, not just an extrinsic bonus. Research would take that seriously.

---

## 6) Promotions/leadership roles are mixed: often satisfying, but also more stressful

The research on promotions and leadership is more mixed than people expect.

In broad terms:
- Promotion/leadership can increase satisfaction because of **status, influence, autonomy, growth**
- But it often also increases **hours, role conflict, stress, administrative load**
- Gains in happiness are often **smaller and less durable** than people predict

For many people, leadership is rewarding only if they actually enjoy:
- coaching
- making decisions under ambiguity
- conflict management
- hiring/performance conversations
- coordination instead of direct problem solving

So the literature does **not** say “leadership = happier.”
It says leadership helps **when it fits your motives and strengths**.

This is especially relevant in research orgs, where moving into management often means doing **less of the technically interesting work itself**.

---

## 7) For creative/research work specifically, intrinsic motivation matters even more

In creativity research (e.g. **Amabile** and related work), intrinsic motivation is strongly linked to:
- creativity
- persistence
- depth of engagement

That is especially relevant for research engineering.  
If you are someone who gets a lot of energy from hard technical problems, “less interesting” work may have a larger negative effect on you than it would on the average worker.

In other words, the general results above may be **more** relevant, not less, for someone in high-skill research.

---

## 8) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the literature for your exact question.

There is **not**, as far as I know, a famous clean body of evidence saying:
> “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paying leadership job regretted it less 10 years later.”

What we do have is indirect evidence:

### a) Long-term regret often centers on the “ideal self”
Research on regret (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) suggests that over longer time horizons, people often regret **not pursuing opportunities aligned with who they wanted to become** more than they regret many specific actions.

That could cut either way for you:
- If your “ideal self” is **technical researcher**, leaving very interesting work may be the bigger regret.
- If your “ideal self” is **leader/builder**, turning down the first real chance to lead may be the bigger regret.

### b) Value misalignment predicts persistent dissatisfaction
The fit literature strongly suggests that choices misaligned with your values/interests tend to generate ongoing dissatisfaction, which is the thing regret is often made of.

So the research-based way to frame regret is less:
- “interesting vs money”

and more:
- **“Which role is more congruent with my core motives?”**

---

# What the evidence seems to imply in your situation

Based on research, I’d summarize your tradeoff this way:

### Reasons to give substantial weight to staying:
- The work is **very interesting**
- Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is a **major predictor of job satisfaction**
- The salary difference is only **a bit higher**
- In already high-paid jobs, extra pay usually has **diminishing returns**

### Reasons to give substantial weight to leaving:
- You’ve “always wanted” to **lead a small team**
- That may reflect deep **person-role fit**, not just prestige
- Leadership may satisfy autonomy/competence/relatedness in a way your current role does not

### What research would warn you not to do:
Do **not** treat this as:
- “interesting work” versus “objective career advancement”

That framing is too crude.

A more research-consistent framing is:
- Which role will give me more of the things that actually drive satisfaction over the next few years:
  - interest
  - autonomy
  - mastery
  - meaning
  - fit
  - relationships
  - growth toward my preferred identity

---

# If you want a very compressed bottom line

**Yes, there is strong research suggesting that doing work you find interesting/meaningful/autonomous is one of the best predictors of being happy in your job.**  
**Salary matters less than most people think, once it is already high and fair.**  
**Leadership can improve satisfaction, but mainly when it fits your genuine motives; otherwise it often adds stress while taking you away from the work you actually enjoy.**  
**Direct evidence on “regret” is thinner, but the best-supported general idea is that people do worse when they choose roles that are misaligned with their core interests and identity.**

---

If you want, I can give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses** on:
1. job satisfaction and interesting work  
2. salary vs satisfaction  
3. leadership/promotion and well-being  
4. regret / person-job fit

answer:
Yes — there is quite a bit of research relevant to this, and the broad picture is fairly consistent:

**Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**  
**Higher pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high.**  
**Whether leading a team will make you happier depends a lot on whether it fits what you personally want, not just whether it looks like “advancement.”**

A research-based summary:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly associated with job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, this usually appears under constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job complexity / challenge**
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**

### Classic and meta-analytic findings
A large literature stemming from **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** finds that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

More modern meta-analyses confirm this. A particularly influential one is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  They found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and complexity** are strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - lower burnout / turnover intentions

In plain English: **people tend to be happier in jobs that are interesting, challenging, and give them control.**

---

## 2) Meaningful work predicts well-being quite robustly

A related literature looks at **meaningful work** rather than “interestingness” per se.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Meaningful work is positively related to:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - **organizational commitment**
  
  and negatively related to:
  - **turnover intentions**

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in knowledge work they often overlap. If your current role feels intellectually alive and important, the literature would predict that this contributes substantially to your well-being.

---

## 3) Pay does increase satisfaction — but less strongly than most people think

There are two related literatures here:

### A. Pay and job satisfaction
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** did a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  The bottom line: **the relationship is positive but modest**.

So higher salary does tend to help, but the effect is usually **smaller** than the effect of job design factors like autonomy, meaningfulness, and engaging work.

### B. Income and overall well-being
The happiness literature on income is more nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income improves life evaluation, with earlier evidence suggesting emotional well-being plateaus at higher income levels.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income on average.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers (2023)** largely reconciled these views: income continues to matter for many people, but with **diminishing returns**, and not equally for everyone.

For someone already in a very high-paying tech role, a **“bit higher salary”** is unlikely, on average, to have as large an effect on day-to-day happiness as changes in:
- how interesting the work is
- autonomy
- who you work with
- whether the role fits your identity

That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant — just that the literature usually doesn’t support **small salary differences dominating well-being** once basic and comfort needs are already far exceeded.

---

## 4) For scientists and research-oriented people, job content often matters enough that they accept lower pay

This part is especially relevant to you.

There is labor-economics research suggesting that people in science/research often accept lower wages for more intrinsically rewarding work.

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**  
  Evidence suggests scientists are willing to accept lower pay for work that offers the intrinsic rewards of doing science itself.

This is not just “nice story” research; it’s one of the clearer pieces of evidence that for research-oriented people, **interesting work is not a luxury add-on — it’s a core part of what they value**.

So if you’re someone who is unusually motivated by intellectually interesting work, you are *not* an outlier in a way the literature would dismiss. The research says that preference is real and economically consequential.

---

## 5) Autonomy is usually one of the biggest levers

Across many studies, **autonomy** is among the strongest predictors of work satisfaction.

This comes both from:
- work design research
- **Self-Determination Theory** literature

A useful reference is:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** on Self-Determination Theory in organizations

Their framework says well-being and motivation are supported when work satisfies needs for:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often supports **competence** and curiosity; leadership can support **autonomy** and influence, but may reduce time spent on the work itself. So the question is not simply “research vs management,” but:

> Which role better satisfies your own mix of autonomy, competence, and relatedness?

---

## 6) “Fit” matters a lot: the best choice is often the one that matches what *you* value most

This is one of the strongest and most practical findings in the literature.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit  
  Better fit is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher commitment
  - lower intentions to quit

This is important because the average result “interesting work predicts happiness” does **not** imply it will dominate for every individual.

If you have a strong longstanding desire to lead a team, then a role that is somewhat less intrinsically interesting but gives you:
- leadership
- growth
- responsibility
- identity alignment (“this is the kind of person I want to become”)

may still produce higher satisfaction because it fits you better.

So the literature does **not** say “always choose the most interesting work.”  
It says:

> People are happiest when their jobs provide the things they personally value most — and for many people, interesting work is one of those things.

---

## 7) What about promotion/leadership? The evidence is more mixed

This is where the research is less neat.

In general, promotions and leadership roles can increase:
- status
- autonomy
- sense of progress
- compensation

But they can also increase:
- stress
- administrative burden
- role conflict
- less time doing the actual work one enjoys

A common finding in job and career research is that **promotion gains often adapt over time**. The “I’ve advanced” satisfaction bump may be real but not always durable.

The literature doesn’t give a simple answer like “management makes people happier.” It tends to show that leadership is satisfying **when it aligns with personal motives and identity**, and less so when people are pushed into management because it’s the obvious next step.

This fits a lot of anecdotal tech reality, but it’s also consistent with the person-job fit literature.

---

## 8) On regret: direct evidence for this exact tradeoff is limited, but there are a few useful findings

I’m not aware of strong research that directly compares:

> “People who chose more interesting work over slightly more money + first-line leadership”

and then tracks regret specifically.

That exact comparison is too narrow for the literature.

But there are two relevant bodies of research:

### A. Regret research
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** and related work found that:
  - in the short term, people often regret actions
  - in the long term, people more often regret **inactions** and missed opportunities

This is broad regret research, not career-specific, but it suggests:
- if **leading a team is a genuine aspiration**, not trying it may become a long-term regret
- if **doing deep interesting work is central to your identity**, leaving it may also become a regret if the new role feels hollow

So regret depends heavily on **which possible self feels more central**:
- the researcher doing unusually interesting work
- or the builder/leader you’ve wanted to become

### B. “Ideal self” / aspiration mismatch
Career research generally finds that dissatisfaction grows when people feel their work diverges from who they want to become. That again points to **fit** more than any single objective factor.

---

## 9) If I had to compress the evidence into one conclusion

A fair research-based summary would be:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is a very strong predictor of job satisfaction and often of overall well-being.**
2. **Salary increases help, but usually modestly once you’re already well-paid.**
3. **Leadership roles are not universally better; they help most when they match a real personal aspiration.**
4. **The most important moderator is fit: which role better matches what you value and who you want to become.**
5. **There is little evidence that people systematically regret choosing more interesting work over slightly more money.**
6. **There is some broader evidence that people later regret not pursuing important aspirations — so if leading is deeply important to you, that matters a lot.**

---

## 10) Applied to your specific situation, but still staying research-grounded

Given your description:

- **Google**: very interesting work
- **Anthropic**: slightly more money, chance to lead a small team, less interesting work

The literature would predict:

### Reasons you might be happier staying at Google
- The work itself is more engaging
- You likely preserve intrinsic motivation
- Day-to-day satisfaction may be higher
- The salary difference alone probably won’t compensate much if the work feels materially less interesting

### Reasons you might be happier moving to Anthropic
- If leading a team is a major aspiration, the role may better fit your identity
- You may gain autonomy, responsibility, and growth
- You might regret *not* trying leadership more than you would regret less-interesting work

So the strongest research-based question is probably not:

> “Which job is objectively better?”

but:

> “Which missing piece would cost me more in well-being over the next few years: less interesting work, or not getting to lead?”

That is very consistent with the literature.

---

## 11) If you want a very short answer

**Yes: there is substantial research suggesting that interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is usually more important for job happiness than modest pay differences, especially among already highly paid knowledge workers.**  
**But if leading a team is a major personal aspiration, person-job fit research suggests that the leadership role could still make you happier despite being less interesting.**  
**Direct regret evidence on this exact tradeoff is sparse, though long-run regret research often points to missed aspirations rather than foregone salary.**

---

## 12) References worth looking up

A compact reading list:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design and outcomes  
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976/1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
- **Stern (2004)** — “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”  
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — long-term regret and inaction

If you want, I can also do one of two things:

1. give you a **more technical summary with the strongest studies and what they actually estimated**, or  
2. build a **decision framework based only on research constructs** like intrinsic motivation, autonomy, fit, expected adaptation, and regret.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research that is relevant, though not usually phrased as “interesting work vs slightly higher salary + first-time management.”

The short version is:

1. **Interesting, challenging, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is much smaller than most people assume**, especially once you’re already well-paid.
3. **Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being and lower burnout.**
4. **Leadership/promotion can increase satisfaction for some people, but it also tends to increase stress**, so whether it improves overall happiness depends a lot on fit.
5. **The best predictor is often person–job fit**: people are happiest when the role matches what they personally value most.

## What the research says

### 1) “Interesting work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied via job characteristics like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **problem solving / complexity**

The classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses confirmed it.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these kinds of intrinsic job characteristics had **moderate-to-strong associations with job satisfaction**, often stronger than many extrinsic factors. In practice, jobs that are more autonomous, varied, and meaningful are reliably associated with higher satisfaction, engagement, and lower withdrawal.

So on your first question — **yes, people in more intrinsically interesting work tend, on average, to be happier with their jobs**.

## 2) Pay matters, but less than intrinsic job quality
There is also a meta-analysis directly on **pay and job satisfaction**: **Judge et al. (2010)**.

The core result is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — roughly in the **small-effect** range. In plain English:

- higher pay does predict somewhat higher satisfaction,
- but **not nearly as much** as job quality, autonomy, meaning, or fit.

This is especially relevant in your case because you’re already at a likely very high income level. For high earners, a “bit higher salary” often improves **life evaluation** (“I’m doing well”) more than **day-to-day enjoyment** of work.

That fits the broader income/happiness literature too:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found income related more strongly to evaluative well-being than emotional well-being.
- **Killingsworth (2021/2023)** argued the flattening is less absolute than earlier thought, but even there, the practical takeaway is still: **money helps, but it is not the dominant driver of day-to-day happiness for already affluent professionals**.

## 3) Meaningful work has a large association with well-being
A more recent meta-analysis on **meaningful work** — **Allan et al.** — found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **life satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **lower burnout**

The association with job satisfaction is not tiny; it’s typically **moderate to large**.

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in knowledge work. If your current role feels genuinely more intellectually alive, that’s not a trivial preference — research treats it as one of the central ingredients of sustainable work well-being.

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to be more important than extrinsic incentives for sustained engagement
A related literature distinguishes:

- **intrinsic motivation**: doing the work because it’s interesting/absorbing
- **extrinsic motivation**: doing it for money, status, title

Across many studies, intrinsic motivation is usually a better predictor of **persistence, engagement, and higher-quality performance** than extrinsic incentives alone.

A meta-analysis by **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)** found that intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality. That’s not exactly happiness, but it matters because people often become less happy in roles where their day-to-day motivation shifts from “this is fascinating” to “this is a good career move.”

## 5) Person–job fit is crucial
There is also strong evidence from **person–environment fit** research that people are happier when their role aligns with their interests, values, and preferred way of working.

Meta-analyses like **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** show that fit is strongly related to:
- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower turnover intentions

This is one reason the literature won’t give one universal answer like “always choose the interesting job” or “always choose leadership.” The better answer is:

- If **leading people** is a central aspiration for you, then the Anthropic role may actually be **more intrinsically meaningful**, even if the technical content is less interesting.
- If what energizes you most is **doing deep research**, then moving away from that may reduce satisfaction even if the title and pay improve.

## 6) What about promotion / leading a team?
The research here is more mixed.

In general, managerial or promoted roles often come with:
- more autonomy,
- more influence,
- more status,
- more pay,

but also:
- more interruptions,
- more emotional labor,
- more responsibility for others,
- more stress,
- less hands-on technical work.

Panel studies of promotions generally suggest:
- promotions often **increase job satisfaction in the short term**,
- but they also often increase **stress and workload**,
- and the effects on **overall life satisfaction** are less consistently positive than people expect.

So the evidence is **not** “becoming a team lead makes people happier.” It’s more like:
- it helps if the person wants that kind of responsibility,
- it hurts if they mainly wanted to keep doing the core work they loved.

That is very consistent with first-time managers in technical fields: some find it deeply fulfilling; others feel they traded away the best part of the job.

## 7) What about regret?
This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of strong research specifically showing “people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying leadership job” or vice versa in the exact way you’re asking.

What the literature does support is this:

- **Mismatch with core interests/values predicts later dissatisfaction and turnover.**
- People tend to regret career moves that take them away from what they see as central to their identity.
- Long-term career satisfaction depends less on maximizing a single variable and more on alignment with one’s enduring motives.

So the evidence on **regret** is more indirect:
- if you highly value intellectually interesting work and give it up, that is a plausible source of later regret;
- if you highly value becoming a leader and defer it repeatedly, that too can become a source of regret.

Research does not say one of these regrets is universally more common.

---

## If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff

### What research would likely weight heavily
For someone in your position, the literature would usually rank these as major determinants of future job satisfaction:

1. **Day-to-day intrinsic quality of the work**
   - Is it stimulating?
   - Does it use your strengths?
   - Are you learning?

2. **Autonomy and impact**
   - Team leadership may increase these, even if technical interest drops.

3. **Meaning**
   - Do you care more about the problems, or about building and leading a team?

4. **Fit**
   - Are you a researcher who wants to remain a researcher?
   - Or a future leader who is ready to trade technical novelty for leverage through others?

5. **Compensation**
   - Important, but usually not the strongest predictor of job happiness once compensation is already high.

## The most defensible evidence-based summary
If I had to compress the literature into one sentence, it would be:

> **On average, people are more satisfied in work that is intrinsically interesting, autonomous, and meaningful than in work that merely pays somewhat more — but leadership opportunities can outweigh that if leadership itself is one of the things they find meaningful.**

So:
- **If the Anthropic role is “less interesting” in the sense of lower intellectual engagement and weaker fit with your core motivations, the research would predict a real risk of lower job satisfaction despite better pay and title.**
- **If leading a small team is itself a long-standing intrinsic goal, then the role may not actually be less meaningful for you overall, even if the technical content is less exciting.**

## A few specific papers worth looking up
If you want the actual literature, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary*
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Allan, B. A., et al.** — meta-analysis on meaningful work and well-being
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — person–environment fit meta-analysis
- **Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014)** — intrinsic motivation vs incentives
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010)** — income and emotional well-being
- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021/2023)** — income and experienced well-being

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more academic summary with effect sizes and paper-by-paper results**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework strictly based on the research** (e.g. how to weigh interest, leadership aspiration, and salary).

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not many studies ask your exact question in the form “interesting Google job vs slightly higher-paid less-interesting leadership role.”

The short version is:

- **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained motivation.**
- **Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction and happiness is usually much smaller than people expect, especially once income is already high enough that basic financial stress is low.**
- **Direct evidence on “regret” is thinner**, but the broader psychology literature suggests people often regret **missed opportunities** over the long run, while organizational research suggests poor fit between the work itself and your motives tends to reduce well-being.

So if you want the research-based answer: **on average, people in more interesting, meaningful, better-designed jobs do tend to report higher work well-being than people who simply earn somewhat more**. But in your case, the leadership opportunity is also a non-monetary good, so this is not just “interest vs salary”; it is really **research interest vs leadership/growth/identity**.

## What the research says

### 1. “Interesting work” is strongly tied to job satisfaction
A foundational literature in organizational psychology is the **job characteristics / work design** literature.

The classic model is **Hackman & Oldham**: jobs are more motivating when they have:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are very close to what people informally mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  found that these kinds of job characteristics are **moderately to strongly associated** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - engagement
  - lower turnover intentions
  - better well-being

In practice, the most powerful features tend to be:
- **autonomy**
- **meaning/significance**
- **variety/complexity**
- **feedback**

So if your Google role is genuinely more intellectually engaging, more aligned with your strengths, and gives more autonomy, the literature would predict **higher day-to-day job satisfaction** there.

---

### 2. Meaningful work is associated with well-being
There is also a newer literature on **meaningful work**.

Across reviews/meta-analyses, people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- lower burnout / lower withdrawal
- sometimes higher general well-being too

This is related to, but not identical with, “interestingness.” Work can be interesting because it is intellectually stimulating, meaningful because it contributes to something important, or both.

Relevant line of work:
- **Allan and colleagues** on meaningful work
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)** on sources of meaning in work
- **Steger et al.** on meaning at work

The general result is robust: **meaning is not a luxury variable**. It has a substantial relationship with how good work feels.

---

### 3. Pay has a positive but usually modest relationship with job satisfaction
This is one of the clearest findings in the literature: **salary matters, but less than people often think**, especially for high earners.

A well-known meta-analysis:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It matters more when:
- you are under financial stress
- the difference changes your lifestyle or security
- the pay signals status/fairness
- the pay difference compounds into much larger long-term wealth

But for someone already well compensated, a “bit higher salary” usually predicts a much smaller happiness change than:
- whether the work is interesting
- whether you feel effective
- how much autonomy you have
- whether your manager/team situation is good
- whether the role fits your identity

Economists often summarize this as a difference between **pecuniary** and **non-pecuniary** job attributes.

---

### 4. People really do trade salary for better work conditions
There is also labor-economics evidence that workers value non-pay job qualities enough to accept lower wages for them. This is the **compensating differentials** idea.

People often accept lower pay for:
- more autonomy
- more flexibility
- more meaningful work
- better colleagues/boss
- lower stress
- shorter commutes
- more interesting tasks

This matters because it is not just self-report (“I say I care about meaning”); it also shows up in **actual choices**.

So the data are broadly consistent with the idea that **interesting work has real utility**, not just moral prestige.

---

### 5. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness matter a lot
Another major research framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (**Deci & Ryan**).

It says well-being is higher when work supports:
- **autonomy**: “I have meaningful control”
- **competence**: “I’m using and developing my abilities”
- **relatedness**: “I feel connected/respected”

This theory has been tested a lot, including in work settings.

A role that is more interesting often scores higher on:
- autonomy
- competence satisfaction

A leadership role may increase:
- autonomy
- competence growth
- relatedness/impact

But if the actual daily work becomes less intellectually satisfying, that can reduce competence enjoyment or intrinsic motivation.

This is important for your case: **leading a team is not “just money.”** It is also a source of autonomy, mastery, and identity satisfaction for some people.

---

## What about regret?

### Direct evidence is weaker
I’m not aware of a strong body of research saying, specifically, “people who choose more interesting work over higher pay later regret it less.” That exact question is not very well studied.

But there are a few relevant findings.

### 1. Long-run regret is often about missed opportunities
A famous result from regret research:
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** and later work  
  suggests that over the long term, people often regret **inactions** more than **actions**.

That would suggest that if “leading a small team” is a genuine long-held aspiration, then **passing on that opportunity** could become a source of regret.

But this is only indirect evidence. It does **not** override the job-design literature.

### 2. Misfit predicts dissatisfaction
A stronger work-specific result is from **person-job fit / person-organization fit** research:
- when your work matches your motives, strengths, and values, you tend to be more satisfied
- when it doesn’t, you see more dissatisfaction, burnout, and turnover intentions

So the likely regret question is not really:
- “more interesting vs more money?”

It is:
- “which option is the better fit with my enduring motives and identity?”

If your enduring motives are:
- “I need intellectually interesting work”
then the less-interesting role is risky.

If your enduring motives are:
- “I want to build and lead”
then passing on the team-lead opportunity is risky.

The literature would predict that **misfit**, more than the salary difference itself, is what leads to later dissatisfaction.

---

## So, do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?

**At work: yes, generally.**
That is one of the more robust conclusions from organizational psychology.

If by “interesting” you mean:
- intellectually stimulating
- varied
- meaningful
- autonomous
- skill-using

then yes, those features reliably predict better work well-being.

**In life overall:** still often yes, but the effect is less clean because overall happiness also depends on:
- stress
- hours
- family
- health
- security
- income adequacy
- commute/location
- manager and team

So interesting work is a strong positive factor, but not the only one.

---

## Which factors seem to matter most, empirically?

Very roughly, for people already earning good money, the research usually puts these near the top:

1. **Autonomy/control**
2. **Interesting/meaningful work**
3. **Good manager and colleagues**
4. **Low chronic stress / manageable workload**
5. **Fit with values and identity**
6. **Security/fairness**
7. **Pay**

That ordering is not universal, but it is much closer to the research than the lay intuition that “more pay must dominate.”

---

## One nuance very relevant to your case
Your decision is not:

- **interesting work** vs **money**

It is more like:

- **interesting research work**
vs
- **leadership opportunity + somewhat more money + less interesting content**

Research would say both sides contain important non-monetary value.

A useful research-grounded decomposition is:

### Google role may give you:
- stronger intrinsic motivation
- more intellectual stimulation
- better day-to-day enjoyment
- stronger “flow” / craftsmanship satisfaction

### Anthropic role may give you:
- leadership identity development
- more autonomy/status
- learning through managing
- maybe stronger long-term career capital if you want to become a leader

So the critical question is:
**Which of these is the more central driver of your well-being over the next few years: engaging work itself, or the growth/identity value of becoming a leader?**

Research cannot answer that personally for you, but it does say:
- **don’t overweight the salary difference**
- **do take very seriously the daily quality of the work**
- **do also take seriously the value of the leadership opportunity if that is a genuine aspiration**

---

## A cautious evidence-based bottom line

If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence:

> For people who are already financially comfortable, choosing the more interesting/meaningful/autonomous job usually predicts more day-to-day work happiness than choosing the slightly higher-paid job; however, a leadership opportunity can also be a major source of satisfaction if it fits a core personal aspiration.

Or even shorter:

- **Interesting work beats a modest pay bump**, on average.
- **But “leading a team” is not just a pay bump**; it may be a deep fit factor.

---

## Good papers / authors to look up
If you want to read the research directly, these are good starting points:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary...*

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)**  
  Job Characteristics Model / *Work Redesign*

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

- **Deci & Ryan**  
  Self-Determination Theory, especially work motivation

- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
  review on meaningful work

- **Allan et al.**  
  work on meaningful work and vocational well-being

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
  action vs inaction regret

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-page decision memo based only on research**, with a weighted checklist like:
- interestingness
- autonomy
- leadership fit
- burnout risk
- compensation
- long-term optionality

so you can use it for this specific choice.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad pattern is fairly consistent:

**The intrinsic quality of the work itself** — things like interest, autonomy, challenge, learning, meaning, and fit with your motives — tends to predict **job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being more strongly than salary level alone**.

A few of the most relevant findings:

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses of work design.

### Main result
Jobs tend to be more satisfying and motivating when they have:
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- some sense of **identity/ownership**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics like **autonomy**, **task significance**, and **variety** were **consistently and meaningfully related** to job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and performance.

In plain English:
> People are usually happier when their work is intellectually engaging, gives them discretion, and feels important.

That lines up closely with what most people mean by “interesting work.”

---

## 2) Meaningful work has a strong relationship with well-being

Another relevant literature is on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** did a meta-analysis and found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - higher **life satisfaction**
  - lower **turnover intentions**

So if your Google role feels substantially more interesting in a way that also makes it feel more meaningful, the evidence suggests that is not a trivial preference — it is one of the stronger predictors of satisfaction.

---

## 3) Salary matters, but less than many people expect

There is direct research on pay and job satisfaction.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** did a meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction**.
- The key takeaway: the relationship is **positive but modest**.

That is important because people often intuitively expect “higher salary” to have a very large effect on how happy they’ll be with a job. The research generally says:

> Pay matters, but it is not one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction once compensation is already good.

This is especially relevant in your case, because the comparison is not “financially insecure vs secure.” It sounds more like **high salary vs slightly higher salary**. Research usually finds the marginal psychological value of that kind of increase is smaller than people anticipate.

Related work in economics and psychology also shows:
- people adapt to income changes
- relative pay and fairness matter a lot
- beyond a point, day-to-day experience of work matters more than another increment of money

So if the salary increase is “a bit higher,” research would generally not expect that alone to dominate the quality of the work.

---

## 4) Person-job fit matters a lot — and this is where the leadership piece becomes important

This is the part that makes your case more nuanced.

You said that **leading a small team is something you always wanted**.

That is not just an “extrinsic perk.” In the research literature, that may count as **better person-job fit** or fulfillment of an important motive.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-environment fit** found that better fit is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher commitment
  - lower intention to quit
  - better well-being outcomes

So the evidence would suggest:

- If you deeply value **leadership**, mentoring, and building a team, then the Anthropic role may fit your motives better in that dimension.
- If you deeply value **hands-on intellectually exciting research**, the Google role may fit better there.

Research would not treat “interesting work” and “leading a team” as totally separate categories. Both can be intrinsically rewarding. The crucial question is:
> Which role better fits the motives that matter most to you?

---

## 5) Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are a strong framework here

**Self-Determination Theory** is one of the most robust frameworks in motivation research.

It argues that well-being is supported when work satisfies needs for:
- **autonomy** — having meaningful control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Work that is “interesting” often boosts **competence** and sometimes **autonomy**.  
Leading a team can boost **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness**.

So from a research perspective, the Anthropic offer is not simply “more money, less interesting.” It may be:
- less intellectually interesting in one way
- but potentially more satisfying in autonomy/leadership/mentoring

That is why fit matters so much.

---

## 6) What about regret specifically?

This part is weaker.

There is **much less direct research** on:
> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying / leadership one?”

Most of the literature measures:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- career satisfaction

rather than explicit regret.

### What the research does suggest indirectly
People tend to do worse when they choose on **extrinsic rewards alone** and ignore intrinsic fit.

There is also a big literature on **affective forecasting** showing that people often mispredict what will make them happy:
- they often overestimate the lasting impact of salary/status differences
- they often underweight the importance of day-to-day lived experience

So while I don’t know of a famous clean study exactly on “regret after choosing the more interesting job,” the proxy evidence points this way:

> Long-term dissatisfaction is more often associated with poor fit and low-quality day-to-day work than with not having maximized salary.

---

## 7) One caveat: workload and stress can swamp everything

Another robust finding from occupational psychology:

High job demands — especially:
- long hours
- role overload
- chronic time pressure
- work-home conflict
- unclear expectations

are strongly linked to **burnout** and lower well-being.

So if one role is much more intense or chaotic, that can matter as much as “interestingness” or pay.

This is relevant because your decision is not just:
- interesting work
vs
- slightly more money + leadership

It may also involve differences in:
- work hours
- pressure
- stability
- management burden
- conflict load
- responsibility without support

The research on burnout would say not to ignore that dimension.

---

# Bottom-line answer from the literature

If I compress the research into one sentence:

> **On average, people are happier in jobs with better intrinsic qualities — interesting work, autonomy, meaning, fit, growth, and good relationships — than in jobs that mainly offer more pay.**

But in your case, there is a crucial nuance:

> **Leading a small team may itself be one of those intrinsic qualities**, if that is something you genuinely value and have wanted for a long time.

So the research does **not** imply:
- “stay at the more interesting IC job no matter what.”

It implies something closer to:
1. **A small salary increase should usually get relatively low weight.**
2. **Interesting/challenging/meaningful work should get high weight.**
3. **Fit with a genuine leadership motive should also get high weight.**
4. **Workload/burnout risk should get high weight.**

---

# If you want the most research-grounded summary

A rough ranking of what tends to matter most for work happiness:

### Usually stronger predictors
- autonomy/control
- interesting/challenging work
- meaning/significance
- person-job fit
- quality of manager/colleagues
- manageable workload / low burnout risk

### Usually weaker than people expect
- absolute pay level, especially when already well-paid

### Can be very important if personally valued
- leadership responsibility
- advancement
- prestige/status

---

# Useful papers to look at

If you want to read actual research papers/reviews, these are good starting points:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

4. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

5. **Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
   *Self-determination theory and work motivation.*

6. For general happiness/adaptation to income:
   - **Clark, Frijters, & Shields (2008)** on relative income and happiness
   - **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** on income and well-being

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **shorter “executive summary”** of the research, or  
2. help you build a **decision matrix based on the literature** for your exact Google vs Anthropic tradeoff.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research around the pieces of this question, though not many studies on your exact choice (“more interesting IC role vs slightly better-paid team lead role at another top lab”).

### Short version

The best-supported finding is:

**Intrinsic features of work** — things like **interest, challenge, autonomy, skill use, meaningfulness, and fit with your values/interests** — tend to predict **job satisfaction and engagement more strongly and more durably** than **pay differences**, especially once pay is already high.

So if by “interesting” you mean **the work itself is more absorbing/intellectually engaging**, research generally says that matters a lot for happiness at work. A modest salary increase usually matters less.  
But: **leadership** can itself be intrinsically rewarding if it gives you more **autonomy, impact, growth, and meaning**, so it is not just an “extrinsic” factor.

---

## What the research says

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually captured indirectly by constructs like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **challenge / learning**
- **intrinsic motivation**

The classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analytic work strongly supports it.

### Key result
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback** had **moderate to strong relationships** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal / turnover intentions

So, on average, jobs that are more interesting, more self-directed, and better designed are associated with meaningfully higher satisfaction.

---

## 2) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another strong literature is **person-job fit** / **person-environment fit**.

If a job matches your **interests, strengths, and values**, people tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- less desire to leave
- better well-being

### Key result
The meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that person-job fit and related fit measures were **strongly associated** with job satisfaction and commitment.

That is especially relevant to your case, because “this work is much less interesting to me” is basically a statement about **weaker fit**.

---

## 3) Meaningful work predicts not just job satisfaction, but broader well-being

There is also a “meaningful work” literature.

### Key result
A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. (2019)** found that meaningful work is positively related to:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **general well-being**

The effect is not trivial. Meaningful work is one of the more robust positive predictors in the career/well-being literature.

If your Google work feels more intellectually meaningful or personally compelling, that is not a minor preference; research suggests it is central.

---

## 4) Pay matters, but less than people expect — especially at already high income

There is a very consistent finding that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

### Key result
A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al.** found that the correlation between **pay level and job satisfaction** is **small** (roughly in the neighborhood of **r ≈ .15**, depending on specification).

That means pay is not irrelevant, but it is much weaker than many people intuitively assume.

In broader well-being research, higher income is associated with higher well-being, but:

- gains are **nonlinear**
- marginal benefit tends to **shrink at higher income levels**
- at already-high salaries, a “bit higher” salary usually does **less** for day-to-day happiness than changes in the quality of daily work

For someone already working at Google/Anthropic-level compensation, research would usually predict that **the content and structure of the job** matter more than a moderate pay bump.

---

## 5) Intrinsic motives tend to produce better well-being than extrinsic motives

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent work on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations**.

Broadly, people do better when their work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

and when their goals are more **intrinsic** than purely about:

- money
- status
- image

There is a large literature showing that environments supporting those basic needs produce better:

- well-being
- motivation
- persistence
- lower burnout

So if your current job gives you stronger feelings of “I genuinely want to do this work,” that is usually a good sign in the literature.

---

## 6) Promotions / leadership often help less than people expect, unless the role itself fits

This is the most nuanced part for your case.

There is some panel evidence that **promotions** increase satisfaction, but often:

- the boost is **partly about pay/status**
- some of it **fades over time**
- added stress and responsibility can offset benefits

So “leading a small team” is not automatically better or worse.

What matters is **why** you want leadership:

- If you want it because it lets you mentor, shape direction, and have more impact, it may increase autonomy/meaning and genuinely improve well-being.
- If it mainly serves as a status marker while the underlying work is much less interesting, the satisfaction boost may be weaker and less durable.

In research terms: leadership can be **intrinsically rewarding** if it matches your values and strengths.

---

## 7) On “regret”: direct evidence is thinner

I don’t know of a strong literature that directly answers:

> “Do people later regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking a slightly higher-paid, less interesting leadership role?”

But the closest evidence points this way:

- choices aligned with **intrinsic interest**, **fit**, and **meaning** tend to produce **more durable satisfaction**
- choices driven mainly by **extrinsic rewards** tend to produce weaker gains than people forecast
- people often **mispredict** what will matter, overweighting salary/status and underweighting daily experience

So while “regret” studies are limited, the broader evidence does **not** suggest that people are generally happier taking less-interesting work for a modest salary bump.

---

# What seems most relevant to your specific decision

For someone in your situation, the research would suggest paying close attention to these variables:

1. **How much less interesting is the Anthropic work, really?**  
   Small difference or big difference?

2. **Would leading a team be intrinsically rewarding to you?**  
   If yes, that may partly offset lower topic-interest.

3. **How much autonomy/control would each role give you?**  
   Autonomy is one of the strongest predictors.

4. **Will you be using your strongest skills?**  
   Skill use / fit matters a lot.

5. **What will your day-to-day week feel like?**  
   Research on happiness often comes down to repeated daily experience, not abstract career labels.

6. **Is the salary difference large enough to materially change your life?**  
   If it’s only “a bit higher,” the literature says its impact on well-being is probably modest.

---

# Bottom-line research summary

If I had to compress the evidence into one sentence:

**On average, people are more satisfied in work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and well-matched to them than in work that is merely better paid — especially when both jobs already pay very well.**

The main caveat is that **leadership itself can be an intrinsic good** if it gives you meaning, growth, and impact.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** with factors like interest, autonomy, meaning, leadership fit, salary, and long-term career capital, weighted roughly according to what the literature says tends to predict satisfaction.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research relevant to this, though usually it is framed as **intrinsic job characteristics** (interesting work, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit) versus **extrinsic rewards** (pay, title, status), rather than exactly your Google-vs-Anthropic scenario.

## Short version

The research literature is pretty consistent on this:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but its direct relationship with job satisfaction is usually much smaller** than the relationship for intrinsic aspects of the job.
- **People tend to adapt more quickly to salary/status gains** than to the day-to-day quality of what they do.
- There is **less direct research on “regret”** specifically, but the indirect evidence strongly suggests that choosing a job with more interesting/meaningful daily work often leads to higher sustained satisfaction than choosing a slightly higher-paying but less engaging job.

So if the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” the literature would generally predict that **the content of the work itself is likely to matter more for long-run happiness at work**.

---

## What the research says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics strongly predict satisfaction

A classic line of research in organizational psychology is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later supported by large meta-analyses.

The main recurring result: jobs are more satisfying when they offer:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- a sense of **meaningfulness**

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson (2007)** found that these work-design features had **moderate to strong associations** with outcomes like:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

The broad takeaway is not subtle: **how the work feels day to day matters a lot**.

---

### 2) “Interesting/meaningful work” usually matters more than pay level

There is a well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** on the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction**. The result is often summarized as:

- the correlation between **actual pay** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**  
- roughly in the neighborhood of **r ≈ .15** from memory

That is not zero, but it is much smaller than the effect sizes usually seen for things like:

- autonomy
- meaningful work
- good management
- person-job fit
- learning/growth

A common interpretation in the literature is:

- **pay matters a lot when it is too low, unfair, or stressful**
- once pay is “good enough” or “fair enough,” **differences in work quality tend to explain much more of job satisfaction**

So a **small-to-moderate salary increase** often does **less** for long-run satisfaction than people expect.

---

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There are also meta-analyses on **meaningful work**. Different papers operationalize it slightly differently, but the overall result is consistent:

Higher perceived meaning in work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- higher life satisfaction
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions

A good general summary is that these associations are **moderate to strong**, often stronger than the effect of pay level.

This matters because “interesting” work often overlaps with:

- meaningfulness
- identity fit
- curiosity
- mastery
- intellectual stimulation

Those are exactly the kinds of intrinsic drivers that the literature repeatedly finds to be psychologically important.

---

### 4) Person-job fit matters

A related literature looks at **fit**: whether your interests and values match the work.

Research on **person-job fit** and **interest congruence** generally finds that people are more satisfied, persist longer, and do better when their work aligns with what they actually enjoy.

The effect sizes here are often **modest rather than huge**, but they are very consistent.

In plain English:  
if you are the kind of person who really cares about intellectually interesting work, that preference itself is not trivial. The literature would treat it as a meaningful predictor of satisfaction, not as a luxury variable.

---

### 5) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

This also matters because you asked about “being happier,” not only “liking the job.”

There is a long literature showing that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are positively linked**. Meta-analyses usually find a **moderate correlation** between them.

That doesn’t prove simple one-way causality, but it does mean that:

- work quality is not “just work”
- if you spend a large share of your waking life doing work that is more interesting and engaging, it often does affect overall well-being

So from a happiness standpoint, “interesting work” is not a niche preference; it is often a major contributor.

---

## What about regret?

This is the part where the evidence is thinner.

I’m not aware of a large, famous literature specifically asking:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership job?”

Most of the evidence uses outcomes like:

- job satisfaction
- burnout
- engagement
- turnover
- life satisfaction
- commitment

rather than explicit retrospective regret.

### What the indirect evidence suggests

Two literatures are relevant here:

#### Affective forecasting / misprediction
People often **mispredict what will make them happier**. They tend to overweight:

- salary
- status
- prestige
- titles

and underweight:

- daily experience
- how interesting the work is
- who they work with
- autonomy and control

#### Hedonic adaptation
People often **adapt** to raises, status changes, and prestige gains faster than they expect.

So the indirect conclusion many researchers would draw is:

- a salary bump may feel important at decision time
- but the **daily quality of the work** often matters more in lived experience over time

That is not the same as saying “everyone regrets taking the higher-paying less-interesting job.” But it does suggest that **people may systematically underweight intrinsic job quality when making career choices**.

---

## One very important complication in your case: “leading a small team”

This is not just “less interesting work for more money.” It also introduces **leadership/management**, which research treats as a qualitatively different role.

That matters because leading a team can increase:

- autonomy
- impact
- status
- influence
- growth
- sense of achievement

Those are all things that can raise satisfaction.

But it can also decrease:

- time spent on core technical/research work
- immersion in intrinsically interesting tasks
- direct craft/mastery
- flow

So the key research question is not simply:

> “Do I like leadership in theory?”

It is more like:

> “Do I enjoy the actual day-to-day activities of management enough that they themselves are interesting/meaningful?”

That distinction is important because many technically strong people say they “want leadership,” but what they really want is:

- more influence
- more ownership
- more recognition

not necessarily the real daily work of management:
- hiring
- coordination
- conflict handling
- performance feedback
- stakeholder alignment
- less uninterrupted deep work

Research on role fit and career orientation generally finds that people are happier when the role matches their dominant motivations. If someone is fundamentally more driven by **technical mastery and research curiosity** than by **people leadership**, moving into management can reduce satisfaction even if it looks like career progress.

---

## If you want the most defensible research-based summary

If I had to compress the literature into a few sentences:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Actual pay level has a positive but relatively small association with job satisfaction**, especially once compensation is already good.
3. **People often underweight day-to-day work quality and overweight salary/title/status** when making career decisions.
4. There is **less direct evidence on regret**, but the best indirect evidence suggests that choosing work that is more intrinsically engaging tends to produce better long-run satisfaction than choosing a slightly better-paid role that is less interesting.
5. The major exception is when the “less interesting” role adds something intrinsically important to you — especially **real enjoyment of leadership, influence, mentoring, or larger impact**.

---

## Representative studies / literatures to look at

If you want papers to read, these are good anchors:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design and outcomes
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- Meta-analytic literature on **meaningful work** and **person-job fit**
- Literature on **self-determination theory at work**: autonomy, competence, relatedness
- Literature on **affective forecasting** and **hedonic adaptation**

---

## Probably the most relevant empirical takeaway for your decision

If the pay difference is only **modest**, and both jobs are already high-quality/high-pay jobs, the literature would generally predict that your long-run satisfaction will depend more on:

- whether the daily work is engaging
- whether it feels meaningful
- whether the role matches your motivations
- whether you actually enjoy the new activity mix

than on the salary difference itself.

So the core research-backed question is likely:

> Which role gives you better day-to-day intrinsic rewards, not just better headline rewards?

If you want, I can turn this into a **more formal mini literature review with citations and 1–2 sentence summaries of each paper**, so you can evaluate the evidence more carefully.
